Tafseer:
يَسْــــَٔـلُوْنَكَ عَنِ الْاَنْفَالِ ۭقُلِ الْاَنْفَالُ لِلّٰهِ وَالرَّسُوْلِ ۚ فَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ وَاَصْلِحُوْا ذَاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ ۠ وَاَطِيْعُوا اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗٓ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُّؤْمِنِيْنَ

*Anfāl* is the plural of *nafl*, which means increase, addition, or surplus. If something is given to someone beyond what they are entitled to, that extra portion is called *nafl*. Similarly, if someone performs more than what is obligatorily required, that additional part is called *nafl*. Here, *anfāl* refers to the spoils of war (*mal-e-ghanimat*) obtained in the battlefield by those striving in the cause of Allah from the defeated enemy. This expression contains a subtle indication that the spoils of war obtained from the enemy are a surplus favor and an additional reward from Allah, because the actual reward of Jihad is preserved separately with Allah in the form of an eternal and boundless reward.

**The Nature of the Question Regarding Spoils:** As we explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, a question is sometimes of the nature of an objection, whether this is obvious from the words or hidden within them. Here, the context indicates that the reference is to a question of this nature. This question, as we indicated above, relates to the spoils of war obtained in the Battle of Badr. Before this, the Muslims had neither faced any organized war with the disbelievers nor had the question of spoils and their distribution arisen. This war took place in the year 2 AH, in which Allah granted the Muslims a splendid victory and a substantial amount of spoils was obtained. In the times of ignorance, the custom was that whoever looted any wealth in war was entitled to it. Based on this custom, some people, particularly the weaker Muslims, raised questions that revealed that the spirit of piety (*taqwa*), mutual well-being, and obedience to Allah and His Messenger—which true faith demands—was not yet fully mature in a group. Although this question, as is clear from the context, was raised only by specific individuals, it pointed to a significant weakness within the Islamic society. For this reason, the Quran began this Surah of purification and organization with this very event, addressing the root of the issue before it could grow. It mentioned it in a general form so as not to expose any specific group, but to have all Muslims collectively accept this teaching and prevent any tendency contrary to piety, trust in God, mutual empathy, and obedience to Allah and His Messenger.

The details of the question referred to here by the Quran are present in the books of history and biography. In Ibn Hisham, it is recorded that after completing the battle, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) ordered all the spoils collected by the army to be gathered together. Consequently, everything was gathered. Then, differences arose among the people as to who was entitled to it. Those who had collected it claimed: 'This is our right; if we had not collected it, it would not have been obtained; we routed the enemy, which is why it came to hand.' Similarly, those who were guarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said: 'We could have done everything—we could have fought and collected spoils—but we prioritized the duty of protecting the Prophet over all other tasks; therefore, others cannot be more entitled to the spoils than us.' In short, various questions arose, bringing to light some hidden weaknesses, and divine wisdom demanded that these weaknesses be cured on the spot before they could grow further.

**The Fundamental Answer to the Questions:** 'Say, "The spoils of war belong to Allah and the Messenger."' This is the comprehensive and fundamental answer to all these questions: tell them that the spoils of war are the property of Allah and the Messenger. 'Property of Allah and the Messenger' is the expression used in the Quran for collective ownership. This fundamental answer put an end to the custom of ignorance regarding spoils, which was the source of the questions mentioned above. Thus, entitlement to spoils will not be based on who collected them, who actually fought, or who guarded; rather, all Mujahideen, regardless of the nature of their service, will share in them, and other Muslims will also have a share. Having given this fundamental answer, the discourse turned toward correcting the weaknesses revealed by this event. Then, ahead in verse 41, the details of this brief statement are explained: how much of it will be distributed among the Mujahideen, and how much will be deposited in the treasury (*Bayt al-Mal*) as the right of other Muslims.

**The Foundation of the Muslims' Collective Integration:** 'So fear Allah and amend that which is between you.' Just as piety and respecting the womb were made the foundation of all familial and social well-being in Surah An-Nisa, piety and amending mutual relations (*islah dhat al-bayn*) are made the foundation of the Muslims' collective integration here. The meaning is: since the spoils of war fundamentally belong to Allah and the Messenger, accept whatever distribution Allah and the Messenger make with complete cheerfulness and satisfaction. Let no doubt or resentment arise in the heart regarding Allah's command, nor any feeling of jealousy or envy toward your religious brothers as to why so-and-so was made a partner in this wealth. All Muslims are brothers to one another; the foundation of their mutual relations is brotherhood, mercy, and love. This is a group characterized by 'merciful among themselves'; therefore, any state of jealousy, rivalry, selfishness, and individual greed is contrary to the faith and piety they have adopted. Those who still harbor any thorn against their religious brothers must remove it, cleansing the repository of their hearts of all dust.

'and obey Allah and His Messenger, if you should be believers.' This states the actual demand of faith in Allah: those who claim to believe in Allah and His Messenger must obey every command of Allah and His Messenger. It is contrary to faith that when a command of Allah and His Messenger is contrary to one's desires, a feeling of rebellion arises or some resentment or doubt finds place in the heart. The words 'if you should be believers' show that those who claim faith must understand this reality of faith. Those who have not understood this reality of faith are completely empty of reality in their claim to faith.

اِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُوْنَ الَّذِيْنَ اِذَا ذُكِرَ اللّٰهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوْبُهُمْ وَاِذَا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ اٰيٰتُهٗ زَادَتْهُمْ اِيْمَانًا وَّعَلٰي رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُوْنَ 

**The Attributes of True Believers:** Now, the true faith and the attributes of true believers are described. This places the picture of true believers before those weaker ones pointed to in the words 'if you should be believers,' warning them: if you claim faith, cultivate these attributes within yourselves; without these attributes, this claim does not benefit anyone.

'Indeed, the believers are those who, when Allah is mentioned, their hearts fear, and when His verses are recited to them, it increases them in faith.' The first sign of true believers is described as possessing the consciousness of Allah's grandeur, majesty, and glory, because of which they constantly fear Him. When the name of Allah is mentioned, when He is recalled to them, or when something is presented to them as God's word, they listen to it with a deep sense of fear and submissiveness. Thus, the first demand of faith is the fear of God, which arises from a correct concept of His grandeur, majesty, justice, wisdom, lordship, and mercy, and it is from this that the piety (*taqwa*) commanded in 'And fear Allah' above is born. It should be kept in mind here that God's mercy and lordship, as we explained elsewhere, also necessitate His justice and His Day of Judgment; therefore, a correct concept of these attributes does not make a servant reckless regarding God, but increases his fear, and the foundation of this fear lies in the love of God.

**The Second Sign of Faith:** The second sign is described as: when Allah's verses are recited to them, it increases their faith. The context proves that 'verses' (*āyāt*) here refers to the commands and laws of God. The meaning is: after believing in God, what they desire and seek most is the knowledge of His preferences, dislikes, pleasures, and commands, and this knowledge adds to their wealth of faith. Faith is like the root, while the etiquettes, commands, laws, and Shariah are like the branches, leaves, and fruits that sprout from this root. Thus, the entire Shariah is the manifestation of faith and the detail of its implications.

From the expression 'it increases them in faith' (*zādathum īmānan*), it also emerges that those who possess faith welcome its requirements and demands with complete cheerfulness when presented to them. They consider these requirements and demands as the fruit of the tree they planted and the harvest of the field they sowed, and just as every farmer is delighted to see his harvest grow and his tree bear fruit, these believers are delighted to see this increase in their faith. This serves as a subtle reference against those claimants to faith who claimed faith, but when its demands arose, their brows furrowed instead of being delighted, as if some calamity had fallen upon them.

**A Point:** It should also be noted here that after affirming faith, every demand—large or small—presented to the believers opens a field of trial and testing, and true believers, instead of being anxious about this test, attempt to win the field; this effort is a natural requirement of their faithful courage. Fulfilling it opens a new door of success for them in every test, making their faith stronger and stronger. This reality is pointed to in Surah Al-Ahzab in the words: 'And when the believers saw the companies, they said, "This is what Allah and His Messenger had promised us, and Allah and His Messenger spoke the truth." And it increased them only in faith and acceptance.' (Al-Ahzab: 22).

**The Third Sign of Faith:** 'and upon their Lord they rely.' This is their third sign: they rely on their Lord—meaning: whatever the demands of faith are, whether hard or soft, whether they harm or benefit their worldly interests, and whether relationships are broken or joined because of them, they believe that success in this world and the Hereafter lies solely in obeying His commands. To the extent that if they have to sacrifice their lives in the path of faith, they believe that this is the path to attain eternal life. They possess complete trust in their Lord: that in whichever command He gave them and whichever trial He put them into, there is absolute well-being for them. No dealing of God with His servants is empty of wisdom, interest, mercy, and blessing. This segment also contains a reference against those unrefined ones who wished to weigh the demands of religion on the scale of their interests, considering those commands contrary to their desires as devoid of wisdom.

**The Fourth Sign of Faith:** 'Who establish prayer, and from what We have provided them, they spend.' This is the fourth sign, which has the status of the 'mother of all attributes,' as we explained elsewhere. This is because these two things—the care for prayer and spending—integrate all the attributes with which faith beautifies the believers. Thus, after mentioning those specific attributes to correct the weaknesses of the group in view, He mentioned these two things which are both comprehensive and protective of all attributes.

'Those are the believers in truth.' That is: those who possess these attributes are the true believers. Those who have entered the ranks of the believers but are devoid of these attributes are mere claimants to faith, not true believers. Thus, what was stated indirectly in the words 'if you should be believers' is now made completely clear.

'For them are degrees with their Lord and forgiveness and noble provision.' The meaning is: those ranks and degrees before God are not for the mere claimants to faith, but for those who attempt to cultivate these attributes within themselves. In this context, the word 'forgiveness' (*maghfirah*) is highly eloquent and encouraging, revealing that although these attributes are what is desired and ranks will be established based on them, Allah is Merciful and Kind, aware of His servants' weaknesses, which is why His forgiveness is present for those errors and shortcomings that are inseparable from human nature.

As we explained elsewhere, the word 'provision' (*rizq*) is a comprehensive word, and the attribute 'noble' (*karīm*) alongside it clarifies that although the servants will receive all provision and favor through the Lord's bounty, this will be accompanied by the honor that everything will be given to them as their right.

**The Nature of the Decrease and Increase of Faith:** This detail clarifies the reality that faith is not a dead, barren tree, but possesses roots, branches, leaves, and fruits. Its metaphor is described in the Quran as: 'its root is firmly fixed and its branches are in the sky'—it is like a tree whose roots are deep in the earth and its branches are spread in the air. Just as it is based on beliefs, it is also comprised of commands and laws; and just as a lush tree receives nourishment from its roots and also from its branches and leaves, faith receives nourishment and strength from both the knowledge of beliefs and the performance of deeds. If a hollow state arises in beliefs, it dries up; and if some disease affects its branches, it becomes weak. For its correct growth, it is necessary to take care of both its roots and its branches. Through this care, it grows, spreads, and bears fruit; and through its absence, it decreases, shrinks, and dies. In our view, this is the reality made clear by both the Quran and the Hadith. If any of our jurists and theologians denied the decrease and increase of faith, their statement can only be applied to the legal faith (*imani-e-qanuni*). Since the law relates solely to outward conditions, the inward being outside its reach, there is no difference in its eyes between the faith of a sincere believer and that of a hypocrite; but this does not mean that in the eyes of reality, the faith of both is equal. Let us content ourselves with this brief indication here; detailed discussion on this will come in its place.

كَمَآ اَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنْۢ بَيْتِكَ بِالْحَقِّ ۠ وَاِنَّ فَرِيْقًا مِّنَ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ لَكٰرِهُوْنَ 

**The Usage of 'Kama':** The particle *kamā* (just as) is sometimes used in the Arabic language to show the similarity of one event to another. In such cases, the similarity is not within the individual words but within the collective structure of the events. *Kadhalika* (thus) appears in this manner in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 143.

In his famous ode, Imru' al-Qays first narrated one of his love stories, and then began describing his other similar stories by stating: 'thus was it with Umm al-Huwayrith before her'—meaning: a similar event had also happened to him with so-and-so. Above verse 151 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the change of the Qiblah was mentioned, followed without any introduction by: 'Just as We sent among you a messenger from yourselves'—this is also a similar style of expression, meaning: by separating your Qiblah from that of the People of the Book, God distinguished you as a distinct nation. This same style of *kamā* is present in the verse under discussion. As mentioned above, those weaker Muslims who objected to the distribution of the spoils obtained at Badr were rebuked. When their weakness was brought under discussion, the demand of teaching and training made it necessary to draw attention to another weakness of theirs, which they displayed when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) intended to leave Medina for Badr. Thus, on that occasion, their error was overlooked because wisdom demanded it; but when a similar error was committed by them again, they were rebuked, and reference was also made to their past error so they would realize where this disease started, and how far it could go if not corrected.

**The Prophet's Exit for Badr Occurred by Divine Direction:** 'Just as your Lord brought you out of your home in truth.' The words 'your Lord brought you out' (*akhrajaka rabbuka*) are clear proof that the Prophet's exit for Badr occurred by the command of Allah. Ahead in verses 42-43, there is an indication that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was shown in a dream the return of the Quraysh caravan from Syria and the arrival of the Quraysh army from Mecca, and the true reality of the invading army was also made clear—showing that in terms of spirit, they would not carry much weight but would be subdued by the Muslims. Indeed, narrations also show that in this very dream, the Prophet was shown the exact places where the leaders of the Quraysh would be killed, and it was in accordance with the direction of this dream that the Prophet left, which is why it is expressed in the words 'your Lord brought you out of your home.' Ahead in verse 42, it will also become clear that it was by virtue of this divine guidance that the Muslims arrived at the well of Badr to face the Quraysh army exactly at the moment when their army was at one end of the valley and the caravan was passing below.

**The Real Purpose of the Exit for Badr:** 'in truth' (*bil-haqq*)—that is: Allah gave this command to exit for a truthful purpose. This truthful purpose is explained ahead in the words: 'Allah intended to establish the truth by His words and to eliminate the last of the disbelievers' and 'That He should establish the truth and abolish falsehood, even if the criminals disliked it.' This shows that from the very beginning, the Prophet's exit was under the command of Allah for a truthful purpose, which was to elevate the religion and eliminate disbelief. Obviously, disbelief could be eliminated only by defeating the Quraysh army, not by looting their commercial caravan; therefore, those narrations in the books of history and biography claiming that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) intended to attack the commercial caravan led by Abu Sufyan on its return from Syria are contrary to the explicit words of the Quran.

**Pointing to a Weaker Group Within the Muslims:** 'and indeed, a party of the believers was averse.' 'A party of the believers' (*farīqan min al-mu'minīn*) refers to that group of weaker Muslims whose character has been under discussion from the beginning of the Surah, and who later raised those questions regarding the spoils of war upon which He commented in the preceding verses. The word 'party' (*farīqan*) also shows that the number of these people was not very large, but was a small group. When these people heard that two groups of the disbelievers were approaching, with one of whom they were to face, they must have immediately perceived that this face-off was to be with the army, not the commercial caravan; consequently, terror fell upon them, and this terror was the result of that weakness of trust pointed to in verse 2 above.

يُجَادِلُوْنَكَ فِي الْحَقِّ بَعْدَمَا تَبَيَّنَ كَاَنَّمَا يُسَاقُوْنَ اِلَى الْمَوْتِ وَهُمْ يَنْظُرُوْنَ 

**Rebuking the Weakness of the Weaker Ones:** As we explained elsewhere, the word *mujādalah* (arguing) means attempting to make the addressee accept one's view through persistent and subtle means. Now, He explained what way they sought to escape when they realized that the face-off was with the army. Through persistent and subtle means, they attempted to turn the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) toward attacking the commercial caravan. Outwardly, they must have tried to present this advice as being in the interest of Islam and the Muslims—claiming that looting the caravan would break the Quraysh economically because the bulk of their wealth was with it, which would help restore the Muslims' current weak financial state. But at the bottom of this advice lay that very fear of theirs which the Quran exposed: 'as if they were being driven to death while they were looking on.' The most dangerous aspect of this advice was that the Prophet's intent, as proven by the Quran's words 'after it had become clear' (*ba'da mā tabayyana*), was well known to them, yet they used all means to make him accept their view. Although up to that stage, as indicated in the upcoming verse, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had not expressed his intent in explicit words for certain reasons, these people were not so dull-witted as not to understand that when there was a commercial caravan on one side and an army approaching on the other, the Prophet's exit could only be to face the army. These people were weak of heart, but they were not so empty of intellect as to be ignorant of the Prophet's temperament and the demands of Islam to establish the truth—failing to understand this simple point: that looting a commercial caravan was not a task for which the Messenger of God and his devoted companions would go out carrying their lives in their hands. Thus, ahead, it will be detailed that neither the leaders of the Muhajirun faced any confusion in understanding the Prophet's intent, nor did the devoted among the Ansar. All perceived in the very first stage what the Prophet's intent was, and rose to fulfill it. Only one group, out of fear of death, continued to invent excuses until the last moment, which is why their conduct was discussed in the Quran, so Muslims would remain alert against these internal trials in future stages.

وَاِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللّٰهُ اِحْدَى الطَّاۗءِفَتَيْنِ اَنَّهَا لَكُمْ 

**A Wise Method to Measure the Congregation's Resolve:** The ambiguity in the expression 'And [remember] when Allah promised you one of the two groups that it would be yours' is meant to reveal that when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) consulted the Ansar and Muhajirun regarding this expedition, he did not express the matter in two-fold terms—such as: 'under the excuse of protecting their commercial caravan, the Quraysh have sent their army to attack us'—but expressed it in an ambiguous manner: 'two groups of the disbelievers are approaching, and Allah will put one of them in your power.' Why did the Prophet choose this ambiguous style? In our view, before embarking on an important expedition, the Prophet wished to evaluate every group to see who stood where. Obviously, if the Prophet had placed the issue before the people in a clear, two-fold manner, both the sincere and the hypocrites would have had to say 'we hear and we obey,' leaving no room for anyone to differ and no possibility for weakness to be revealed. You will recall that he adopted the same method during the Battle of Uhud as well, placing the question before the people whether they should fight outside the city or remain within it, without expressing his own view. Consequently, the devoted gave their opinion to fight outside, while the weaker ones gave theirs to remain within. This gave him the opportunity to evaluate the strong, the weak, the sincere, and the hypocrites of the congregation. With the same wisdom, the Prophet expressed the matter in an ambiguous manner on this occasion too, so the people's answers would reveal how each was thinking. Thus, he first sought to know the view of the Muhajirun. They clearly understood what the Prophet's intent was. Consequently, Miqdad bin 'Amr among them rose to deliver a speech whose echo will always remain in the history of Islam. He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, proceed to what Allah has commanded you; we are with you. We will not say to you what the Children of Israel said to Moses: "Go, you and your Lord, and fight; indeed, we are sitting right here," but our statement is: go, you and your Lord, and fight; as long as even a single eye of ours turns, we are present to sacrifice our lives.'

Can it even be imagined that any individual represented by these words could harbor any doubt of attacking a commercial caravan?

Having received assurance from the Muhajirun, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) repeated the same words. The Ansar understood that the Prophet now wished to know their view. Consequently, the leader of the Ansar, Sa'd bin Mu'adh, rose and asked: 'Is the Prophet's address directed to us?' Then he delivered that speech whose every word is a warrior's chant, and whose faithful heat has not cooled even after 1400 years. He said: 'We believed in you and confirmed your truth. We bear witness that the religion you brought is the truth. We made a covenant of hearing and obeying with you. Therefore, O Messenger of Allah, proceed to what you intended. By the God who sent you with the truth, if you take us to the bank of this sea and plunge into it, we will plunge into it with you, and not a single man among us will remain behind. We do not fear facing our enemies tomorrow; we will remain steadfast in war. We will be truthful at the time of clash, and perhaps Allah will show you through our hands what will cool your eyes; therefore, in the name of Allah, honor us with your companionship.'

Reflect: Can these speeches belong to those who were planning to attack an unarmed caravan of forty men? And then reflect: Is it not clear from every word that despite the ambiguity of the Prophet's question, both the Ansar and Muhajirun knew as clear as day what the Prophet's intent was and where he was heading? Only one group, as is clear from the Quran's words, was of the weak in faith, who, despite the clarity of the reality, wished to turn toward the caravan because it was unarmed, so there would be no danger and a soft morsel would come to hand. Addressing them, He stated: 'and you wished that the one at which no power of harm [i.e., unarmed] was should be yours' (*wa-tawaddūna anna ghayra dhāti al-shawkati takūnu lakum*). *Shawk* and *shawkah* mean a thorn in Arabic; from this, the word *shawkah* came to be used for weapons, power, and awe. Since the commercial caravan was unarmed, it was expressed through the words *ghayra dhāti al-shawkah*.

**An Intellectual Criterion to Understand Divine Commands:** 'and Allah intended to establish the truth by His words and to eliminate the last of the disbelievers.' This states a natural and intellectual criterion to understand and evaluate the intention of Allah's commands: every command and intention of Allah is for the purpose of establishing truth and abolishing falsehood; therefore, in defining the target of His words—even if they are ambiguous—it is not permissible to ignore this principle. Those who desired to attack the caravan failed to realize how God could intend something through which neither the truth would be elevated nor the power of disbelief eliminated. The word 'words' (*kalimāt*) carries an aspect of ambiguity. Since the matter was expressed before the people in an ambiguous manner on this occasion, the Quran expressed it through the word *kalimāt*. It is indeed those statements containing some briefness and ambiguity whose target is difficult to define; in such situations, the path of believers must be to choose that aspect which aligns with the attributes and glory of Allah, not what is contrary to them. Through 'to eliminate the last of the disbelievers' (*yaqta'a dābira al-kāfirīn*), the Quran also pointed to the reality that the root of all disbelief was the congregation of the Quraysh, which was what was to be eliminated, and this is what Allah could intend, yet one group sought to become champions by attacking the commercial caravan.

'That He should establish the truth and abolish falsehood, even if the criminals disliked it.' This explains the purpose of 'eliminate the last of the disbelievers': that Allah intended to eliminate the last of these disbelievers specifically to elevate the truth and abolish falsehood. God has no personal feud with anyone, but establishing truth and abolishing falsehood is the requirement of His attributes, and God has now made this decision, and this task will be accomplished even if these criminals dislike it.

**The True Picture of the Battle of Badr in the Light of Quranic Indications:** The picture of the Battle of Badr that emerges in the light of the Quranic indications is entirely different from the one presented in the books of history and biography, which orientalists have further distorted.

The Quran informs us that neither the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) nor the Muslims harbored any plan to attack the Quraysh's commercial caravan. The entire plan to attack Medina was made by the Quraysh, who used the excuse of protecting their commercial caravan. The Quraysh were highly fearful of the Muslims gaining ground in Medina. Aside from religious animosity, they feared that the commercial highway between Mecca and Syria was no longer secure for them. For this reason, since the Prophet's emigration, they had been looking for an excuse to destroy the Muslims before they could become a power. Now, either the leader of the caravan, Abu Sufyan, perceived some imaginary danger of a Muslim attack and sent a messenger to summon the Quraysh, or a conspiracy already existed among the Quraysh leaders. In any case, upon Abu Sufyan's message, a heavy army left Mecca for Medina. It was at this stage that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was informed through a dream that two groups of the disbelievers were approaching, with one of whom the Muslims would face.

Following this, the Prophet intended to leave Medina for Badr, and to evaluate the congregation's resolve, he placed the situation before them in an ambiguous manner: two groups are approaching, and one of them will fall into our power and be defeated by us. As soon as the issue was presented, both the Ansar and Muhajirun understood that the Quraysh army was approaching, and they were to face it. Consequently, their leaders expressed their devotion and sacrifice to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) with full enthusiasm. Only a small group among them exerted their effort to turn toward the commercial caravan because it was unarmed, so no danger would be faced and a rich spoil obtained. To expose this group, the Prophet had presented the matter in an ambiguous manner, so those harboring weakness would reveal it, and the sincere would be distinguished from the hypocrites before the battle. Ahead, in the light of some verses of this Surah, we will also show that the Jews had also supported the Quraysh in this war, but withdrew when they saw the battlefield, as is their habit.

The upcoming subject (Verses 9 to 19): Next, Allah referred to those unseen supports that appeared on this occasion to help the Muslims and encourage them, so their faith and trust in Allah's promises and the future of Islam would be strengthened. The mention of the weaker group above was for the collective reform of the Muslims; now, He reminded of those aspects that were to be useful in future stages. Along with encouraging the Muslims, He also warned the disbelievers of the Quraysh: this defeat you suffered is merely the beginning; if you persist in this path, expect harsher days ahead. You made the outcome of this war the criterion of truth and falsehood, and its decision is before you. If your eyes still do not open, and you commit further mischief, remember that We are still here. Your pride in abundance of numbers and means will remain useless, and you will face defeat again; We are on the side of the believers. In this light, recite the verses:

اِذْ تَسْتَغِيْثُوْنَ رَبَّكُمْ فَاسْتَجَابَ لَكُمْ اَنِّىْ مُمِدُّكُمْ بِاَلْفٍ مِّنَ الْمَلٰۗىِٕكَةِ مُرْدِفِيْنَ 

'Indeed, I will reinforce you...' is the explanation of 'and He answered you' (*fastajaba lakum*), and the active participle is used to express the absolute certainty of Allah's promise. *Irdāf* means succession—namely: appearing one after another. This describes the very first divine support that appeared on this occasion. The number of Muslims in this war was very small—namely: only 313, and they were unarmed as well. On the other side, the disbelievers were around one thousand, fully equipped with weapons and means. Under such circumstances, the sole support of the Muslims could only be the help of God; consequently, every individual was in a state of humility, neediness, and constant supplication. To estimate the nature of these supplications, it is enough to read the words of the Prophet's supplication reported in the Ahadith. Obviously, when the unarmed Muslims laid out their hearts before their Lord, these supplications could not be deprived of acceptance. Thus, they were accepted, and the glad tiding was revealed from Allah: do not fear, I will reinforce you with one thousand angels. The meaning is: why do you worry about one thousand disbelievers, when you have one thousand angels in your vanguard? The form of these angels' appearance was described as division after division and rank after rank. It should be kept in mind that in the psychology of warriors, those who see reinforcement after reinforcement arriving in their support find their courage doubled, while the enemy's nerves give way. This subject passed in Surah Ali 'Imran, verses 125-126; take a look at it there too.

وَمَا جَعَلَهُ اللّٰهُ اِلَّا بُشْرٰي وَلِتَطْمَىِٕنَّ بِهٖ قُلُوْبُكُمْ 

The referent of the object pronoun is that promise of help mentioned above. The meaning is: on this occasion, your Lord made this explicit promise of help beforehand solely because you were fearful, so your courage would be restored and your hearts find reassurance. Do not assume that this help of God is restricted to this specific occasion; rather, whenever the believing servants of God go out for Jihad in His cause, seeking His help with faith and sincerity, He will help them, whether they receive glad tidings beforehand or not. This clarification was necessary because an explicit promise of help on the spot can occur only through the Prophet and during his presence; how then could those who came after the Prophet's era find reassurance of the heart? To eliminate this doubt, He stated that this promise was not for that specific occasion alone, but is eternal for the believers.

'and victory is not but from Allah. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.' This is because help, whenever it comes, comes only from Allah. Therefore, let those who rely on God always place their trust in Him; He will always help them. God is Exalted in Might and Dominant; no one has the power to hold His hand. But He is also Wise, which is why if the believers ever face an setback, some wisdom operates within it and some benefit for the servants is hidden at its bottom. This subject was detailed within the discussion of the defeat at Uhud in Surah Ali 'Imran.

Some of the poor of intellect in this era have deduced from this verse that the promise of sending an army of angels was merely to give Muslims a boost so they would muster the courage to face the disbelievers. In their view, the Quran itself exposed this secret after the war, stating that this was said merely for your reassurance and had no reality. Thus, they claim that first Allah deceived the Muslims, and then Himself exposed His deceit—warning them: I deceived you this time to make you fight, but do not rely on it in the future, as there are no such things as angels. Perhaps these gentlemen do not consider Allah to be as intelligent as themselves.

اِذْ يُغَشِّيْكُمُ النُّعَاسَ اَمَنَةً مِّنْهُ وَيُنَزِّلُ عَلَيْكُمْ مِّنَ السَّمَاۗءِ مَاۗءً لِّيُطَهِّرَكُمْ بِهٖ وَيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمْ رِجْزَ الشَّيْطٰنِ وَلِيَرْبِطَ عَلٰي قُلُوْبِكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتَ بِهِ الْاَقْدَامَ 

This describes the second divine support in this war, and mentions that night on the morning of which the battle took place. The present-preterite verb is used to bring the situation to life, which is a well-known usage. He stated: This was also from Allah: that during the night He cast sleep over you, so your nerves and minds found rest and you rose in the morning fresh and ready for the battle. This sleep is attributed by Allah to Himself, because for those whose small group is to face a massive army in the morning, falling asleep even for a short time is indeed a manifestation of divine support. Sleep is lost at the slightest worry, let alone a worry like the one the Muslims must have had on that occasion; but those who receive the strokes of God's reassurance can sleep even on the scaffold. Thus, the Muslims slept during the night, and this gave their nerves, hearts, and minds such rest that they rose fresh for the battle. Under Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 154, we wrote that for an army, merely finding the opportunity to sleep is not enough; rather, its true effect depends on the state of the heart and mind, and this is not granted to everyone, but only to those over whom the Controller of the Hearts casts His tranquility.

**Eliminating a Misunderstanding:** Generally, people have understood that this state of drowsiness fell upon the Muslims during the very heat of the battle, to the extent that swords were falling from their hands. But this is completely incomprehensible. First, it is strange that Allah would count a state as His favor when its benefit goes entirely to the disbelievers; what greater support could there be for them than having the Muslims fall asleep during the battle, even if for a very short time? Second, this is contrary to the explicit words of the Quran. This type of sleep is mentioned in two places in the Quran: in Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 154, and here. The words of Ali 'Imran are: 'Then after distress, He sent down security upon you - sleep overcoming a party of you, while another party cared only for themselves...' In this verse, the distress refers to the defeat at Uhud; therefore, since the sleep occurred after this distress, how can its occurrence belong to the time of battle? It could only occur after the battle had ended. We discussed the context and significance of this sleep in the commentary of Ali 'Imran.

In this verse of Al-Anfal, this sleep is mentioned among those supports that appeared before the actual battle began. Above, you saw the reference to the glad tiding of sending an army of angels; obviously, this glad tiding was given before the battle. In the subsequent segment, the descent of rain is mentioned; everyone knows that this also occurred before the battle. Why then would something belonging to the heat of the battle be placed between these two? The Quran, through its structure of expression, has made the context of the event beautiful, but the misfortune is that people do not reflect on the Quran. A doubt may arise: the Quran used the word *nu'ās*, which means drowsiness or a light nap; if a deep sleep of reassurance were intended, the word *nawm* or some other synonym would have been used. In our view, this doubt carries no weight. First, in deep worry, what a person is deprived of is indeed that initial drowsiness; if it comes in any way and the eye closes even for a moment, the person manages to sleep. God cast this over the Muslims, as is clear from the verb *yughashshīkum*, which is why they slept. Second, on a journey or in the battlefield, no sane person sleeps a deep, careless sleep; whatever sleep they sleep is a light nap. Therefore, in our view, the Quran used this word in a highly appropriate and eloquent manner.

'and sent down upon you from the sky, rain by which to purify you and remove from you the evil suggestions of Satan.' This refers to the third divine support: that at the exact moment, Allah sent down rain for you from the sky. The words 'from the sky' (*mina al-samā'*) are highly meaningful. Narrations show that the disbelievers had arrived first to occupy the water-well, because of which the Muslims were in deep anxiety regarding water. Through 'from the sky,' Allah drew the Muslims' attention to His special concern: when the disbelievers attempted to deprive you of the earth's water, they could not harm you; your Lord sent down water for you from the sky. In 'to purify you' (*li-yutahhirakum*), the benefit of water described sheds light on the taste and inclination of the Companions: that faith and Islam had transformed their values. That water is for drinking is known to everyone, and indeed beasts also know it. In the eyes of a believer, the primary benefit of water is that it is a means of purification and a way to remove the evil suggestions of Satan, and this is highly beloved to Allah. The Companions must have reflected on the issue of water on that occasion, and the concern that must have dominated their minds—more than the need for drinking—was: how will wudu be performed? What will be done for purification? What if ghusl is required? Because of this specific anxiety of theirs, which was a manifestation of their faithful zeal, Allah mentioned these spiritual benefits of water, overlooking its general physical benefits which are known to all. *Rijz al-Shaytan* refers to satanic whispers. Its mention also has a specific context: when a person is in a state of impurity, just as flies gather on filthy things, satanic whispers dominate him more. This is also reported in some Ahadith. Furthermore, if the question of the unavailability of water—an indispensable thing—arises during battle, Satan can use it to spread such discouragement and despair that many a faith would shake.

'and to make firm your hearts and thereby make firm [your] feet.' 'He bound his heart' (*rabata Allāhu 'alā qalbihi*) means: He made his heart firm, giving him patience and steadfastness. Generally, commentators have counted this steadfastness of heart and feet under the rain mentioned above, interpreting this segment from that perspective. My inclination is that this is the detail of the benefits of the sleep mentioned above. My reasons are as follows:

First, the repetition of the particle *li* in *li-yarbit* is a grammatical indication that it is not coordinate with *li-yutahhirakum bihi wa-yudh-hiba 'ankum rijza al-shaytān*. Had it been so, it would have appeared without the repetition of *li*, just as *wa-yudh-hiba* did. In eloquent Arabic, this is the style of expression, which is supported by Arabic literature and Quranic parallels. In this book, multiple examples have passed. In Al-Baqarah: 'Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship and [wants] for you to complete the period and to glorify Allah for that [to] which He has guided you; and perhaps you will be grateful.' (Al-Baqarah: 185). We explained under this verse that these are different reasons described for different commands, which is why *li* is repeated with each, and the structure of expression is not descending but ascending—that is: rising from the bottom to explain the purpose of each command. On this exact principle, the structure here is ascending. The mention of water is at the end: first its benefit is explained, and then the benefit of sleep mentioned above is explained, using the repetition of *li* to indicate that its connection is not with the nearest thing but with the other.

Second, steadfastness of heart, mental peace, and firm feet are clearly connected to sleep; indeed, the Quran termed it *amanah* (security). The reality is that if the night was spent in sleeplessness and anxiety, the mind wanders, and the heart remains scattered and anxious. In such mental and heart anxiety, a person cannot perform even the smallest task with skill, let alone facing the enemy in battle—especially in the war of that era where success depended not on the power of machines, but on the alertness and strength of the warriors' nerves. It should also noted that multiple Arabic poets have described in their war odes: we did not let our enemy sleep during the night, because of which their hearts were so scattered in the morning that their feet could not stand before us.

اِذْ يُوْحِيْ رَبُّكَ اِلَى الْمَلٰۗىِٕكَةِ 

'[Remember] when your Lord inspired the angels...' 'The angels' here refers to that army of angels whose glad tiding was given above. This army was directly under the command of the Lord of Hosts, which is why it received commands directly from Him, and the means for these commands was divine inspiration (*wahy*), because even with their high status, angels do not possess direct access to God without it.

'"Indeed, I am with you, so strengthen those who have believed."' This is the first command this army received. It was stated: I am with you, so make the believers steadfast. One thing known from this is that without God's companionship, even angels can do nothing. Second, the angels' duty was not to tell the Muslims: sit aside, and we will fight to win the field for you; rather, their duty was to make the believers steadfast. Thus, the primary thing was the Muslims' own bravery and steadfastness. If the Muslims show this quality, God's help is with them. The law of God is that His hands always operate from behind the veil of means.

'"I will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieved..."' This means that once the believers display their steadfastness, it will not be long before I cast terror of the Muslims into the hearts of the disbelievers. It should be kept in mind that an army's true strength lies in its courage. If the courage remains intact, the soldier fights even without weapons; but if the courage is broken, the army flees, leaving massive stores of weapons for the enemy. Therefore, stating 'I will cast terror into their hearts' is not a minor thing, but is an expression of breaking their back.

'"so strike [them] upon the necks and strike from them every fingertip."' This is a highly realistic expression of the result of their terror. The meaning is: once their courage is broken, cut them down like sheep, goats, or vegetables. Strike them upon their necks, and strike their every joint; this is the picture of their helplessness caused by terror. As long as the opponent has strength, he rarely gives you the opportunity to strike him wherever you wish; but when his nerves are broken, you may strike him on his head, and he will not be able to utter a word. Specifying the target of the strike also carries a sense of humiliation and degradation of the enemy, pointing to his helplessness.

ذٰلِكَ بِاَنَّهُمْ شَاۗقُّوا اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ ۚ وَمَنْ يُّشَاقِقِ اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ فَاِنَّ اللّٰهَ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ 

This explains why God will cast terror into their hearts and why they will be cut down like vegetables by the Muslims: because they rose to oppose Allah and His Messenger, and those who rise to oppose Allah and His Messenger are subjected by Allah to a severe punishment. Within human nature, no justification exists to fight against God and His Messenger. Justification exists only when fighting occurs against some right; therefore, those who rise for such ignorance may rise like a storm, but when faced with opposition, they settle like bubbles, because their courage has no foundation in any truth.

ذٰلِكُمْ فَذُوْقُوْهُ وَاَنَّ لِلْكٰفِرِيْنَ عَذَابَ النَّارِ 

While the address above was to the Muslims, this suddenly turned to the Quraysh, warning them: this which befell you at Badr is a cash payment, so taste it, and wait for the punishment of Hellfire. This is like an explanation of 'Indeed, Allah is severe in penalty'—warning them that the punishment decreed for you from God must not be assumed to end with this; the actual place of punishment is Hellfire. Wait for it.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اِذَا لَقِيْتُمُ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا زَحْفًا فَلَا تُوَلُّوْهُمُ الْاَدْبَارَ 

**The Meaning of 'Zahf':** The literal meaning of *zahf* is crawling or walking on knees. From this, it came to be used for a massive, heavy-equipped army marching for war, because it also moves slowly. It should noted that this usage belongs not to this machine age, but to the era when the movement of armies occurred through horses, donkeys, and camels.

**Two Well-Known Methods of War in Arabia:** Two methods of war were well known in Arabia: one of organized army marching, and the second which is called guerrilla warfare in this era. The principle of guerrilla warfare was: attack, loot, and run. They called it the war of advance and retreat (*karru wa-farru*). For this, small groups would go out, raid, and hide in their sanctuaries. It possessed no specific code, but whichever way a successful raid could be made and one's self protected represented its true skill.

An organized army march was completely different. It possessed a code that the army had to follow, and the two fighting sides respected it. In the verse, what is under discussion is indeed this organized army march, which is why the word *zahfan* is used, to clarify that this ruling does not relate to guerrilla warfare.

**An Essential Instruction Regarding Future Wars:** Now, Muslims are instructed regarding future wars: when you face the enemy in the form of an organized army march, do not turn your backs. This instruction is based on those divine supports mentioned above: for those whose back is supported by God and His angels, it is forbidden to turn their backs to the the enemy.

**The Severity of the Sin of Turning One's Back in War:** 'And whoever turns his back to them on such a day...' He stated: Those who turn their backs to the enemy under such circumstances will return with the anger of God, and their refuge is Hell. This shows that this sin is equal to disbelief and apostasy. The severity of this sin is based on the fact that the one who flees from the battlefield sometimes creates a severe danger for the entire army, and indeed the entire nation, through his cowardice.

'unless swerving [as a stratagem] for war or joining [another] company'—that is: those forms which a soldier chooses as a stratagem for war, or when a situation arises where he moves from one position to join another of his own positions, are excluded. The meaning is: what is prohibited is turning one's back as a flight; retreating as a stratagem of war is excluded.

فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوْهُمْ وَلٰكِنَّ اللّٰهَ قَتَلَهُمْ ۠ وَمَا رَمَيْتَ اِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلٰكِنَّ اللّٰهَ رَمٰى ۚ وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ مِنْهُ بَلَاۗءً حَسَـنًا ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ سَمِيْعٌ عَلِيْمٌ 

In 'And you did not kill them,' the address is general to the Muslims, and in 'And you threw not' (*mā ramayta*), it is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), which is why there is a difference between the plural and singular. *Ramy* means throwing arrows, throwing pebbles, and throwing dust or ashes. Narrations show that when the armies faced each other, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) took a handful of dust from the earth and threw it toward the disbelievers, saying: *Shāhat al-wujūh* (may their faces be disgraced). *Shāhat al-wujūh* is a phrase of curse in Arabic, and throwing dust on someone has been an ancient method of cursing. It is mentioned in the Torah, and is also found in Arabic traditions. In the commentary of Surah Al-Fil, Maulana Farahi discussed its references.

It should also be noted that sometimes, negating an action is not meant to negate the action itself, but to negate attributing those splendid results that appeared through the veil of the action to the action itself. Having a handful of unarmed Muslims cut down the massive, iron-clad army of the Quraysh like vegetables, or having a pinch of dust thrown from the Prophet's hand turn into a storm that made the disbelievers worry about their own eyes—these were not the achievements of the Muslims' tattered swords or the Prophet's throw. Rather, they were the achievements of that hand of the unseen hidden within the Muslims' scabbards and the Prophet's sleeves. 'So-and-so performed excellently in war' (*ablā fulānun fī al-harbi balā'an hasanan*) means: he displayed his bravery excellently, to the extent that everyone acknowledged him. 'Allah tested His servants excellently' (*abliya Allāhu 'ibādahu balā'an hasanan*) means: Allah displayed His servants' excellent qualities. The coordinate (*ma'tūf 'alayhi*) of *wa-liyubliya al-mu'minīna* is omitted according to the rule of Arabic grammar, as it is clear from the preceding words. Opening this omitted coordinate: that Allah might display the glory of His help and display the Muslims' qualities excellently.

'Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.' Referencing Allah's attributes of hearing and knowing is meant to assure the Muslims that God is not ignorant of anything. He hears the supplications and cries of His servants at all times, and knows their needs and hunger at every moment. The timely appearance of His support at Badr is a fresh witness to this.

ذٰلِكُمْ وَاَنَّ اللّٰهَ مُوْهِنُ كَيْدِ الْكٰفِرِيْنَ 

When *dhālikum* (that) appears in this manner, it stands in place of an entire sentence, and the subsequent particle relates to the hidden concept within it. The meaning is: the glory of God's help and support that appeared is for you in cash, and furthermore, God will continually render weak every plot the disbelievers conspire against you. If you reflect, you will find that this sentence stands in direct opposition to 'That [is for you], so taste it, and indeed, for the disbelievers is the punishment of the Fire' above—meaning: for the disbelievers, this defeat they suffered at Badr is in cash, and for you, this great victory you attained is in cash; ahead for them is Hell, and for you is the glad tiding that all their conspiracies will be scattered, and the true religion will be elevated.

**The Battle of Badr was a Conspiracy of the Disbelievers:** The words 'the plot of the disbelievers' (*kayd al-kāfirīn*) show what we pointed to previously: that this war was the result of a conspiracy by the Quraysh leaders. Under the excuse of protecting their commercial caravan, they attacked the Muslims. But Allah, as will be clear under verse 42 ahead, informed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) of this conspiracy in time, and the Muslims prepared for defense. For this reason, the Quran expressed it through the word *kayd*, which means a plot or conspiracy. Ahead in verse 48, it will be proven that the Jews were also partners in this conspiracy.

Having clarified the individual parts, take a look at the context: this explains why you must not show cowardice or turn your backs, because you do not fight, but God fights on your behalf. God actually fights, though He provides the field for you so your qualities are displayed and you attain the elevation of this world and the Hereafter.

اِنْ تَسـْتَفْتِحُوْا فَقَدْ جَاۗءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ ۚ وَاِنْ تَنْتَهُوْا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَاِنْ تَعُوْدُوْا نَعُدْ ۚ وَلَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْكُمْ فِئَتُكُمْ شَـيْــــًٔـا وَّلَوْ كَثُرَتْ ۙ وَاَنَّ اللّٰهَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ 

In this verse, the Quraysh are addressed directly: Say, what do you say now? You used to say that whoever won this war would be considered on the truth; the victory is before you. It should noted that in their pride of numbers, the Quraysh leaders delivered highly boastful speeches on this occasion. Since they were completely certain of their victory, they made this war the scale of decision—claiming that whatever decision this scale gave, they would submit to it. Abu Jahl was most active in bringing about this war. His supplication is recorded in the books: 'O Allah, whichever of the two sides is the greater transgressor in severing relations, crush him tomorrow.' The Quran took their boastful statements, warning them: if the decision depended on the victory of this war, the Judge's decision has been passed. It should noted that because of this specific character of the Battle of Badr, the Quran termed it *Yawm al-Furqān*—the Day of Decision between truth and falsehood. Ahead in verse 32, it will be clear that the Quraysh openly and insolently said: 'If this call of Muhammad is the truth, let God rain stones upon us or let some other painful punishment fall upon us, then we will believe.'

In 'and if you desist, it is better for you,' there is both advice and censure. The meaning is: it is better for you to learn a lesson; if you do not, you will harm yourselves, not Us. In 'and if you return [to war], We will return [to punishing you],' there is an open threat: if you commit such mischief again, remember that We are not gone. We will be here again to crush your head. 'and never will your company avail you at all, even if it should be numerous.' The meaning is: the only thing you can think of is to attack with greater power and means in the future; so this will also not benefit you. It will only provide more fuel for Our furnace.

'and that Allah is with the believers.' This segment is the soul of the entire verse, and within its words lies a world of threat for the disbelievers and glad tidings for the believers. He stated: Now let whoever wants to come come, and fight whoever wants to fight, and gather whatever numbers they can; We are on the side of the believers!

*What grief is there even if the entire world is hostile,*
*it is sufficient if the One God is on my side.*

The upcoming subject (Verses 20 to 28): The subject of teaching, reforming, purifying, and organizing the Muslims is advancing through its gradual stages, and those whose weaknesses triggered this discourse are given open warnings, while the Muslim society collectively is warned: be aware of your front and back; the evils and virtues of society are shared. If some people attempt to cause a trial, and others do not hold their hands, that trial will ultimately envelop both the good and the bad. Stand firm on the obedience of Allah and His Messenger, avoiding the path of those Jews who claimed before their Messenger 'we believe' though they did not believe. The Prophet's call is a call to true life; accept it with heart and soul. Those who hear and understand the Prophet's call but fail to open their hearts to it find the law of God intervening between them and their hearts, closing them forever.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اَطِيْعُوا اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ وَلَا تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ وَاَنْتُمْ تَسْمَعُوْنَ 

**Addressing All, But Directing the Discourse to Specific Persons:** Although the address 'O you who have believed' is general in terms of words, the focus of the discourse is on those weaker and hypocritical people whose mention has been running. The general style of the Quran is to address the faults of the weaker ones and hypocrites through general words, so as not to humiliate them, and to let them reform if they wish. As for the good ones, they benefit from it. Thus, the errors of others provide further means to strengthen their own knowledge and action.

**Turning Away From the Messenger is Turning Away From Allah:** In 'obey Allah and His Messenger,' the verb is used in its complete sense—meaning: obey Allah and His Messenger in the manner that faith demands. 'and do not turn away from him while you hear.' That is: in the very presence of the Messenger, while you hear his call with both ears, do not turn away from him. The words 'while you hear' (*wa-antum tasma'ūna*) show the gravity of their conduct: when you stumble in the presence of the Messenger, what will be your state in the future? Those who fall in the full light of day possess no excuse for their fall. The pronoun in 'from him' (*'anhu*) returns to the Messenger, although both Allah and His Messenger are mentioned above. This shows that turning away from the Messenger is equivalent to turning away from Allah. Whoever turns away from the Messenger has turned away from Allah. The sole means of connecting with God and obeying Him is His Messenger.

**An Indication to the Jews:** 'And do not be like those who say, "We have heard," while they do not hear.' That is: do not be like those who claim 'we hear and we obey' while their action is 'we hear and we disobey.' Although the matter is expressed in indications, every reader of the Quran can perceive that this indicates the Jews. The Quran has made it clear in Surah Al-Baqarah that the Jews say 'we hear and we obey' while their action is 'we hear and we disobey,' and has also clarified that this conduct of theirs occurred in the very presence of their Messenger. Thus, the weaker Muslims are subtly informed: this conduct of yours is not following the Messenger, but following the Jews. If you claim faith, do not walk on the footsteps of this cursed nation.

اِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَاۗبِّ عِنْدَ اللّٰهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِيْنَ لَا يَعْقِلُوْنَ 

While the severity was hinted at in the preceding segment, it has become completely open in this segment.

**The Worst of Beasts Before God:** He stated: The worst of beasts before God are the deaf and dumb who do not use intellect. The meaning is: if you hear everything but remain deaf and dumb, ignoring the Prophet's words and failing to use intellect and understanding, you are the worst of beasts before God. The Quran has termed the Jews and polytheists the worst of beasts in many places, explaining that they refused to hear and understand. Obviously, whichever group adopts their path belongs to them. We explained elsewhere that man's primary distinguishing attribute is his hearing and understanding. If he deprives himself of this attribute, he is merely a two-legged beast, and the worst of beasts at that. The worst of beasts because a beast, no matter how bad, remains upon its instinct, possessing a value and utility in its place; but when man deprives himself of his distinguishing attribute, even Satan holds his hands over his ears; if he uses intellect and insight, there is no limit to his elevation, just as there is no bottom to his decline if he deprives himself of them.

وَلَوْ عَلِمَ اللّٰهُ فِيْهِمْ خَيْرًا لَّاسْمَعَهُمْ ۭوَلَوْ اَسْمَعَهُمْ لَتَوَلَّوْا وَّهُمْ مُّعْرِضُوْنَ 

**Eliminating a Doubt:** This answers a doubt that may arise in some minds: if human humanity depends on hearing and understanding, and deprivation of it is so disliked by God that He terms such people the worst of beasts, why does He not open their ears with His power and remove the veils from their intellect? In response, He stated: If Allah had found any good within them, He would have made them hear and understand. He did not find this capacity within them, which is why He did not grant them the facilitation. This points to that divine law concerning guidance and misguidance which we have explained repeatedly: that God granted every human the capacity to distinguish between good and evil; those who keep it alive and use it receive further guidance, progressing step by step in knowledge and action; as for those who do not use it, they are not only deprived of further guidance, but the law of God is that the guidance they received from nature is also seized from them. Jesus explained this beautifully: how can a master entrust a treasure of one hundred thousand to a servant who proved a thief with a single penny?

'and if He had made them hear, they would have turned away, while they were averse.' That is: if God had forced guidance into them without this capacity, He could have done so, but it would not have taken root. Their natures would have rejected it, and they would have vomited it out. No matter how healthy a food is, if the stomach is corrupted, it does not accept it; the person swallows the morsel, but vomits it out quickly. For the correct growth of a plant, it is not enough that the seed itself is healthy, but the fertility of the soil is also required. If a gardener plants an excellent seed in barren soil, he may plant it, and the seed may live for a few days based on its inherent strength, but when it does not receive its required nourishment from the soil, it will ultimately dry up. The exact same is the case with the seed of goodness and guidance: it grows in the soil of a healthy nature. If a person's nature has become barren, the power of God can place this seed within him, but His own law is that it cannot grow.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا اسْتَجِيْبُوْا لِلّٰهِ وَلِلرَّسُوْلِ اِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيْكُمْ ۚ وَاعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّ اللّٰهَ يَحُوْلُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهٖ وَاَنَّهٗٓ اِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُوْنَ 

**The Prophet's Call is a Call to True Life:** It passed above that man's humanity depends on his intellect and insight, and his life consists of the life of his spirit and heart. If he is empty of thinking and understanding, and deprived of hearing and accepting the word of truth, he is dead in terms of his purpose of creation. Thus, the Quran has termed the disbelievers dead in many places. The call of Allah and His Messenger is a call to true life, through accepting which the sight receives insight. Through it, the intellect receives that light which removes the veils of the secrets of the universe and the self. Through it, the heart receives that life which turns a piece of flesh into a mirror of God's lights and manifestations. He stated: Accept the call of Allah and His Messenger, because through it you receive the true and eternal life. Jesus explained this: man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from God. This subject was explained under Surah Al-An'am, verse 122: 'And is one who was dead and We gave him life...'

**A Highly Severe Warning:** 'and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart and that to Him you will be gathered.' This is a highly severe warning to those who fail to appreciate a call to good—especially the life-giving call of the Prophet—becoming deaf and dumb. He stated: Remember, those who adopt this path find the law of God intervening between them and their hearts. God intervening means His law intervening. We explained in multiple places in this book that those things occurring under the law and established path of God are sometimes expressed by Allah as His direct action. This was detailed under 'Allah has sealed their hearts'. The meaning is: the hearts of such people become so uncontrolled that they can no longer bring them under control. When a person's heart pursues evil, up to a certain limit, his state is such that if he alerts himself and wishes to stop it, he can stop it and turn it to the path of reform; but once the heart goes beyond this limit, and the person fails to bridle it, he no longer has his hand on the reins or his foot in the stirrup, but becomes helpless before his heart. The Prophet's call is the most effective call to open the eyes, ears, and hearts; therefore, those who close their ears to this call fall under the grip of that divine law which the Quran expressed as 'sealing of the hearts' or 'rust'. Here, the Quran warned the weaker and hypocritical people of this very thing: opportunity still remains; if you wish to reform, you can; if you do not, and continue to feed your diseases, your hearts will become so distorted and sealed that no polishing will affect them. Furthermore, remember that the matter does not end here; you must also be gathered before God. Reflect on the states and outcomes of that Day.

وَاتَّقُوْا فِتْنَةً لَّتُصِيْبَنَّ الَّذِيْنَ ظَلَمُوْا مِنْكُمْ خَاۗصَّةً ۚوَاعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّ اللّٰهَ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ 

This address is to the entire society in general, and to those who prioritized individual reform over caring about what others do in particular. Shaking them, He stated: Do not remain indifferent to the evils arising within your society, but attempt to correct them to the extent of your capability and opportunity, because if an evil takes root in society, it gradually takes the form of a general epidemic, and when it does, its evil effects and outcomes do not remain limited only to those actually involved in those evils, but those who are pleased with or silent over them also fall under their grip, even if they are not practically involved in them.

This reality was explained by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) through the metaphor of passengers on a ship: if some people travel on a ship, some on its upper deck and some on its lower deck, and the lower ones, feeling that they have to suffer the trouble of going up for water, decide to make a hole in the lower part of the ship, and the upper ones, thinking that they are making a hole in their own part and it does not concern them, remain silent, then because of this action of the lower ones, the ship will sink, taking both the upper and lower ones with it.

On this basis, Islam laid the responsibility of preventing others from evil upon everyone. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) stated: 'Whosoever of you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand; if he cannot, then with his tongue; if he cannot, then with his heart—and that is the weakest of faith.' If you look at the context of this verse, it seems that one group consists of those who claim to accept the truth with their tongues, but lack the courage to fulfill its demands. The Quran warned them that if they do not reform their behavior, the law of God will intervene between them and their hearts. The second group consists of those who accept the truth, but do not love it enough to tolerate the displeasure of others for its sake. The Quran warned them that if an evil spreads in society, they will not remain safe from its evil consequences. Only those who fulfill their duty of advice to the extent of their capability will remain safe, regardless of whether anyone listens to them or not, and whether they are pleased or displeased.

'and know that Allah is severe in penalty.' This is an expression of the rigid and impartial nature of the law of recompense. The meaning is: just as God's laws are rigid in physical and material things, they are rigid in moral things. The one who breathes in polluted air is inevitably affected by its pollution, unless he takes the necessary precautions; similarly, the one who spends his life in an evil environment falls under its evil consequences, unless he observes those conditions mentioned above. In this matter, the divine law does not show partiality or favor to anyone.

وَاذْكُرُوْٓا اِذْ اَنْتُمْ قَلِيْلٌ مُّسْتَضْعَفُوْنَ فِي الْاَرْضِ تَخَافُوْنَ اَنْ يَّتَخَطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ فَاٰوٰىكُمْ وَاَيَّدَكُمْ بِنَصْرِهِ وَرَزَقَكُمْ مِّنَ الطَّيِّبٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُوْنَ 

**Allah's Favors Upon the Muslims in the Early Phase of Islam:** To strengthen the demand of obeying Allah and His Messenger, He listed His favors. The purpose is: in the path to which Allah and His Messenger have invited you, you have seen that He supported you at every step. You were few, weak, and oppressed, fearing that the Quraysh would snatch you away; but God sheltered you in Medina, reinforced you with His special help at Badr, and opened the paths of pure provision for you. All these demand that you be grateful and active servants of His religion. Do not fear that by walking on this path, you will fall into some danger. He who has dealt with you so well up to now will not deal with you poorly in the future.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تَخُوْنُوا اللّٰهَ وَالرَّسُوْلَ وَتَخُوْنُوْٓا اَمٰنٰتِكُمْ وَاَنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

This verse stands in direct opposition to verse 24. What was demanded there is protected here from its contrary, and the gratitude whose duty was recalled in 'perhaps you will be grateful' is emphasized here by avoiding what is contrary to it. We explained elsewhere that the true essence of gratitude is recognizing the right of God, confessing it, and fulfilling it with sincerity. Its obvious requirement is that a person should not commit unfaithfulness and betrayal after claiming 'we hear and we obey' to Allah and His Messenger, but must fulfill this covenant under all circumstances. Every covenant is a trust; therefore, its violation, whether secret or open, is betrayal (*khiyānah*).

'and betray your trusts while you know.' This style of expression is similar to Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 42: 'And do not mix the truth with falsehood or conceal the truth while you know.' We explained there that not repeating the negative particle is a grammatical indication that mixing truth with falsehood necessitates concealing the truth; similarly, here, although this segment is under 'Do not betray Allah and the Messenger,' He did not repeat the negative particle, which reveals that betraying Allah and the Messenger is what opens the way for people to betray their own trusts and responsibilities. The word 'trust' (*amānah*) was discussed in detail under Surah An-Nisa, verse 58, showing that the Quran uses this word in a highly broad sense: all rights and responsibilities—whether arising from a covenant or recognized under the natural law of rights and duties, or those capacities and blessings entrusted to man—fall under the meaning of this word. He stated: Do not betray these trusts knowingly. The segment 'while you know' (*wa-antum ta'lamūna*) in this context reveals that those pointed to were committing this act knowingly, and the purpose of this segment is to censure them; nonetheless, it is also a reality in itself that in the Shariah, an action becomes a crime only when committed with knowledge and will.

وَاعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّمَآ اَمْوَالُكُمْ وَاَوْلَادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ 

This points to the core disease: those who are weak in their love and obedience to Allah and His Messenger are, in reality, trapped in the love of wealth and children. When the love of wealth and children dominates to the extent that a person avoids his duties to the One by whose favor he received wealth and children, then wealth and children become a trial (*fitnah*). Whether it is unfaithfulness to God and His Messenger or betrayal of the rights of others, if you track down its causes, at its bottom will lie the excessive love of these two. In this regard, they represent the greatest door to hypocrisy, and from this aspect they are termed a trial. The way to protect oneself from this trial is to keep in mind that great reward which Allah has preserved for those who do not commit unfaithfulness to God and His Messenger out of love for wealth or children, but when faced with a test where on one side is the pleasure of God and His Messenger and on the other is the love of wealth and children, they always lean toward God and His Messenger. He stated: 'and that Allah has with Him a great reward' for such loyal ones.

The upcoming subject (Verses 29 to 40): Next, He encouraged the Muslims: if you remain firm on the covenant of God, not showing weakness because of the love of wealth and children, the time will soon come when He will display a criterion (*furqān*) for you, and all those veils that obstruct the manifestation of the truth will be torn.

Afterward, He referred to His excellent design which He chose to save His Messenger from the joint conspiracy of the disbelievers and open the way of emigration (*hijrah*) for him. This emigration became the preface to the dominance of Islam and the introduction to the decision between truth and falsehood. Up to then, the disbelievers had been mocking the warning of the Quran, asking: if this call is the truth, why does Allah not send a punishment upon us? But Allah did not send a punishment upon them because Allah does not send a decisive punishment upon a nation while the Prophet is present among them; but now that the Prophet and the believers have emigrated, no obstacle remains to prevent the punishment from coming.

Then, the reasons why the disbelievers deserve punishment are described. The delusion they harbored—thinking that because of their guardianship of the House of Allah and performance of certain religious rites, they were upholding the religion of God—is shown to be at its end. The spending they carry out to block the servants of Allah from His religion and to defeat this religion will soon become a source of regret for them; they will face defeat in this world, and in the Hereafter, Allah will gather them all to cast them into Hell.

Finally, He threatened the Quraysh: it is better for them to change their path and accept this call; otherwise, remember that they will face the same end faced by previous nations who denied their messengers. Along with this, Muslims are instructed: continue to fight them until there is no more trial, and the religion on this earth belongs entirely to Allah. Allah is your Ally and Helper. In this light, recite the verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ يَجْعَلْ لَّكُمْ فُرْقَانًا وَّيُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّاٰتِكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيْمِ 

**The Meaning of 'Furqan':** *Furqān* refers to anything that distinguishes between truth and falsehood. This distinguishing thing can be internal or external, intellectual or practical. The Quran has termed proofs and arguments *furqān* in Al-Baqarah verse 185, because they distinguish between truth and falsehood. Indeed, the Quran itself, and the original Torah, are termed *furqān* in multiple places, because Allah revealed these books to distinguish between truth and falsehood. Similarly, in verse 41 of this Surah, the Battle of Badr is termed *furqān*, because this war practically demonstrated who was on the truth and who was on falsehood.

**The Promise of Dominance to the Muslims:** In this verse, a promise is made to the Muslims: if you remain firm on piety—meaning: if you do not commit breach of covenant and unfaithfulness to Allah and His Messenger—Allah will soon bring a time when all the dust on the horizon will be cleared, and the truth will dominate so clearly that the enemies' eyes will be dazzled. The context indicates that this word points to the complete dominance of Islam and the Muslims. Although a *furqān* had appeared in the Battle of Badr, which the Muslims had witnessed, falsehood still surrounded the Muslims from left and right, because of which one group was in a state of hesitation, and this hesitation fell them into those weaknesses discussed above. To firm these hesitant people on the truth, He stated that the time is not far when all these clouds of falsehood will be cleared, and the sun of truth will shine before you with all its brilliance—on the condition that you remain firm on the obedience of Allah and His Messenger and on piety.

'and remove from you your misdeeds.' Here, 'misdeeds' (*sayyi'āt*) refers to those minor errors and shortcomings that are inseparable from human nature. He stated: if you avoid major sins, Allah will not apprehend you for minor lapses, as He is of great favor. Avoiding major sins expiates minor ones.

وَاِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا لِيُثْبِتُوْكَ اَوْ يَقْتُلُوْكَ اَوْ يُخْرِجُوْكَ ۭوَيَمْكُرُوْنَ وَيَمْكُرُ اللّٰه ۭوَاللّٰه خَيْرُ الْمٰكِرِيْنَ 

The literal meaning of *ithbāt* is to bind or restrict, which includes imprisonment.

**Pointing to the Conspiracies of the Quraysh:** This refers to that conspiracy which the Quraysh leaders hatched in Dar al-Nadwah to bring an end to your call to truth. For this, different leaders presented different proposals. The books of history and biography show that after discussion, they agreed on the proposal of murder, and the plan devised was that all major families of the Quraysh would share in the murder, so the Prophet's family would not muster the courage to demand retribution.

**The Meaning of 'Khayru al-Makirin' (The Best of Planners):** 'But they plan, and Allah plans. And Allah is the best of planners.'
