The word 'plan' (*makr*) was discussed under Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 54, where we explained what its meaning is when attributed to Allah. In 'the best of planners,' one aspect is that God's plan always dominates the conspiracies of others. The second is that although the enemy may outwardly view this plan as his own victory, it ultimately opens broad doors for the victory of truth. The best example of this is the emigration itself. After the Prophet's emigration, the Quraysh took a sigh of relief: at last, the thorn of the side was gone; but circumstances soon proved that the thorn was not gone, but the soul of their national body was gone. After that, Islam received a free environment to grow, and the Quraysh, as detailed ahead, were left waiting for their inevitable destruction.

**Further Emphasis of the Promise of Furqan:** Recalling this event is meant to emphasize that promise of *furqān* mentioned in the preceding verse. The meaning is: if you wish to see how God, through His design and carsazi, turns unfavorable circumstances into favorable ones, there is no need to go far; your Prophet's life is the greatest witness to it. A time was there when the fate of Islam was attached solely to the Prophet's person and a few helpless individuals. The Quraysh decided to murder the Prophet to end the entire matter. They conspired jointly, but Allah chose such a design that the Prophet emerged throwing dust into the eyes of his mortal enemies, and no one could harm him. Furthermore, it was not merely that the Prophet emerged, but circumstances proved that the sun emerged from the eclipse. The Quraysh assumed that when the Prophet was exiled from his nation to go among an alien people, his call would die on its own in an alien environment; but Allah showed through His design that the most fertile soil for the growth of the plant of Islam was indeed the soil of Yathrib, to which the disbelievers themselves drove Islam. God brings His schemes to fruition in this manner. The enemy assumes he won the game, but in reality, God's turn succeeds. Through the hands of the enemies of truth themselves, He brings about that task in which the victory of truth and the death of the enemy are hidden. Therefore, do not fear the current unfavorable circumstances; place your trust in God, the best of planners. From behind these darknesses, a great light and *furqān* is to emerge.

**An Eloquent Style of Address:** Although the address in 'And [remember, O Muhammad], when those who disbelieved plotted against you...' is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), in its context, it carries two aspects. In one aspect, it connects with the preceding subject; in the second, it serves as an introduction to the upcoming subject, in which the Prophet is informed: the reason why we did not send a punishment upon the Quraysh while they were demanding it was because you were present among them; but now that you have emerged from among them, what can save them from Our punishment? They have deprived themselves of the shield of security. Since Allah chose to clarify to His Prophet on this delicate occasion what his status is before Him, the demand of eloquence made it necessary to address him directly: do not grieve because they exiled you; they were living solely because of you; now they will see how We treat them. Simultaneously, this gave the Ummah the glad tiding of the appearance of that *furqān* pointed to above.

وَاِذَا تُتْلٰى عَلَيْهِمْ اٰيٰتُنَا قَالُوْا قَدْ سَمِعْنَا لَوْ نَشَاۗءُ لَقُلْنَا مِثْلَ هٰذَآ ۙ اِنْ هٰذَآ اِلَّآ اَسَاطِيْرُ الْاَوَّلِيْنَ ۔ وَاِذْ قَالُوا اللّٰهُمَّ اِنْ كَانَ هٰذَا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَاَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنَا حِجَارَةً مِّنَ السَّمَاۗءِ اَوِ ائْتِنَا بِعَذَابٍ اَلِيْمٍ 

This subject passed under Surah Al-An'am, verse 92. These verses are introduced here to show how the Quraysh were challenging God, and what boasts they were uttering to check the Prophet, yet Allah did not send a punishment upon them in response to their challenge, because of which their audacity grew, and they became even bolder in presenting their falsehood as truth. In their arrogance, they failed to realize why We granted them respite despite their repeated demand: 'O Allah, if this should be the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky...' Today, let them hear the clear answer.

وَمَا كَانَ اللّٰه لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَاَنْتَ فِيْهِمْ ۭ وَمَا كَانَ اللّٰه مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوْنَ 

This is the answer to the question mentioned above, and since the purpose on this occasion is specifically to console the Prophet, the address is to him. He stated: Allah was not to send down a punishment upon them while you are among them. This points to that divine law which Allah has established regarding sending punishment upon a prophet's nation—namely: as long as the prophet is present among his nation, he is like the heart within the body. He continues to ask forgiveness for them from his Lord, and invites them to seek forgiveness. His presence among them is witness that some life remains within the nation. Through his call, life is given to those who possess the capacity to accept the life of faith, and their seeking of forgiveness also serves as a shield for the nation. No matter how much mischief the wicked of the nation commit, and how much they challenge Allah and His Messenger, Allah overlooks them, and if He puts them into some calamity, its nature is of warning, not of decisive punishment. But when the nation's rebellion reaches the point of plotting the murder of the prophet and making life difficult for the righteous, Allah commands the prophet and his companions to emigrate, after which the nation is deprived of that security they enjoyed because of the prophet and the righteous. Then, one of two things must occur: if those believing in the prophet are only a few numbered individuals, after their emigration, the punishment of God falls upon the nation, clearing the earth of their filthy existence, because sustaining absolute falsehood is contrary to the nature of this universe and the wisdom of its Creator; and if those believing in the prophet are a substantial number—as was the case with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—clash begins between truth and falsehood, and falsehood, clashing with truth, is ultimately shattered and abolished, and Allah displays the *furqān* of truth. 'The truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed is falsehood, [by nature], ever bound to depart.' 'and Allah would not punish them while they seek forgiveness'—that is: although after the Prophet's emigration, security has been lifted, and nothing remains to save them from punishment, if they seek forgiveness, God will save them from punishment. This is like softening the severe threat in the preceding segment: opportunity still remains for them to change their path, ask forgiveness of God, and save themselves from His apprehension. Thus, after the threat, this is an invitation to the Quraysh to seek forgiveness. Ahead, this same concept is expressed in the words: 'Say to those who have disbelieved that if they cease, what has previously occurred will be forgiven for them.' (Al-Anfal: 38).

وَمَا لَهُمْ اَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللّٰه 

'But what [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them...' This is a blow to that vain pride of the Quraysh which they harbored due to their guardianship of the Kaaba and performance of certain associated rites. It has been a common misfortune of nations that they distorted the religion of God to fit their desires, yet gave such significance to these distorted rites that they considered themselves the benefactors of the religion and the beloved of God, assuming that while they were performing these services, God could not lay His hand upon them. In this trial the Jews fell, in this the Christians perished, the same deviation befell the Quraysh, and with great sorrow it must be said that in this very trial Satan also fell this Ummah. The Quraysh assumed that since they were the guardians of the Kaaba, performing the duties of providing water (*siqāyah*) and food (*rifādah*), and were the descendants of Abraham, they possessed every claim to be honored and near to God. Because of this pride, the Prophet's warnings of punishment were highly heavy upon them. They considered themselves the protectors of the Kaaba, and assumed that the Kaaba was also their protector.

'while they obstruct [people] from the Sacred Mosque...' He stated: How can this vain pride of guardianship save them from punishment when their deed is that they block those servants of Allah from this House who are most entitled to it, who are the bearers of the original religion of Abraham, and who wish to restore this House with the call of monotheism and the worship of the One God? This points to the Muslims, who were being exiled not only from this House but from the city in which this House is located.

'and they were not [fit to be] its guardians. Its guardians are not but the righteous, but most of them do not know.' This negated their claim to guardianship: how are they its guardians? Its guardians can only be the righteous servants of Allah. This points to the fact that they are betrayers, rebels, and destroyers of the purpose of this House's construction and its sanctity; therefore, neither do they have any right to dominate it, nor should they expect any favor from God in its name. The true guardians of this House are Our righteous servants who have been blocked from it by these wrongdoers.

At this stage, remember what passed under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 124: when Allah promised Abraham to make him a leader of a great nation, Abraham asked whether this promise would also apply to his descendants; in response, Allah stated: 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.' This covenant of My leadership belongs to the righteous among your descendants; the wrongdoers are not included. The center of this leadership of Abraham was designated as the Kaaba; therefore, by virtue of Abraham's prayer and Allah's clarification, it was decided at the very time of this House's construction that its guardianship and the leadership of Abraham's descendants would belong only to those who remain firm on the monotheism of Allah, guarding His limits, and respecting His covenant—not to those who have wiped out the honor of Abraham's religion and the Kaaba. Here, also remember those words Jesus addressed to the scribes and priests claiming the guardianship of the Temple: 'you have made my Father's house a den of thieves.'

'but most of them do not know' points to that general ignorance in which the Arabs were embroiled at that time. Due to a long period spent in the darkness of ignorance, they had completely forgotten their original history. They preserved only as a matter of national pride that they were the descendants of Abraham and Ishmael; beyond this, they had no knowledge of why Abraham came to this land, what his call was, what the distinguishing characteristics of the religion he advocated were, why he settled Ishmael here, why the Kaaba was constructed, and what trusts and responsibilities were entrusted to Ishmael's descendants regarding this House. Several social customs and rites of Hajj that had come down from Abraham's time had changed so much that distinguishing between the original and the alteration was difficult. They had turned the Kaaba into their national temple, whose key-holding and various administrative departments were inherited among different families, of which they were proud as if of an ancestral property. The Quran pointed to this ignorance of theirs: they claim the guardianship of the Kaaba, but have no knowledge of what the Kaaba is and what the conditions of its guardianship are.

وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ عِنْدَ الْبَيْتِ اِلَّا مُكَاۗءً وَّتَصْدِيَةً ۭ فَذُوْقُوا الْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُوْنَ 

This is an example showing that they are neither worthy of the Kaaba's guardianship nor have any knowledge of the purpose of this House's construction. The purpose for which Abraham constructed the Kaaba was detailed in Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, take a look at the following verses of Surah Ibrahim:

'And [remember] when Abraham said, "My Lord, make this city secure and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols. My Lord, indeed they have led astray many among mankind. So whoever follows me - then he is of me; and whoever disobeys me - indeed, You are [yet] Forgiving and Merciful. Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your Sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful."' (Ibrahim: 35-37).

If you reflect on the words of this prayer, it becomes clear why Abraham settled Ishmael in Mecca, what he supplicated for his descendants, what the purpose of constructing the Kaaba was, and what his target was in settling Ishmael's descendants near this House. Particularly, the words 'that they may establish prayer' (*liyupīmū al-salāh*) are witness that just as the Kaaba was made the center of Allah's worship, settling Ishmael's descendants near this House was fundamentally for the establishment of prayer. But just as the Quraysh turned the Kaaba into a temple of idols, they completely wiped out the honor of prayer, for which they were settled here. He stated: What is their prayer? Whistling and clapping. *Mukā'* means whistling or making sounds with the mouth; *tasdiyah* means clapping. The Quran, through its highly eloquent expression, has shown from the very form of their worship that this mockery can have no connection to prayer. Look at the form and do not ask of the state. We explained elsewhere that to evaluate what is of the religion and what is not, there are both internal and external criteria. A person possessing religious taste can recognize many innovations from their outward form alone, knowing that these absurdities can have no connection to the religion. The rites of mourning (*ta'ziyah-dari*), the absurdities performed at shrines, and the practices of the gatherings of innovators are not things whose absurdity requires scientific research to decide; every person of taste recognizes at first glance that these things can have no connection to the religion. In every word of the religion, there is a reflection of dignity, gravity, humility, fear of God, and purity, and a fragrance of wisdom, knowledge, and spirituality. Anyone possessing insight and some life in their spiritual smell knows merely by looking and smelling that a certain thing is not of the religion. Scientific research comes after this, and its means are different. Here, the Quran has shown that this is the prayer of those claiming the guardianship of the Kaaba, whose form itself bears witness that it is an invention of Satan. It carries not the slightest reflection of that prayer for whose establishment they were settled here, and for whose sake the House of God was entrusted to them.

**Components of Pagan Worship:** This making of sounds, whistling, blowing of horns, and ringing of bells are all of the same class. These have been components of pagan worship since ancient times. It is difficult to research what concept lay behind them; it is possible these actions were chosen to drive away spirits. We explained elsewhere that to establish their religious leadership over the entire peninsula, the Quraysh committed the cleverness of gathering the idols of all tribes within and around the Kaaba. Obviously, when these idols came, the rites and customs of their worship also came. Ultimately, the abrahimi prayer was entirely excluded from the Sanctuary, to the extent that no one even knew it, while the foolishnesses of whistling and clapping remained.

'So taste the punishment for what you disbelieved.' The discourse regarding the Quraysh's crimes, which began in the third person above in 'But what plea have they...', now turned to address them directly, and the eloquence in this address in terms of threat is obvious. The meaning is: remove this delusion from your minds that you are the guardians of the Kaaba. Do not praise yourselves. A whole history of your disbelief, betrayal, and breach of covenant has been written; so now, taste the punishment of God. This also points to that defeat they suffered at Badr, and is a threat of those blows that are to fall upon them in the future. The meaning is: the blows of God have started to fall upon you; taste them one after another and count them.

اِنَّ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا يُنْفِقُوْنَ 

Now, He clarified that the enthusiasm present among the Quraysh leaders to destroy Islam, and the wealth they are spending with liberality in this enthusiasm, will not prevent the *furqān* of truth from appearing. This clarification was necessary because, in reality, the Quraysh leaders displayed great liberality at Badr—every leader attempting to outdo the other in providing weapons and provisions for the army. This made an impression on the weaker Muslims: the Quraysh possess abundance of numbers, weapons, and wealth, and display great enthusiasm in spending; how can a few unarmed Muslims stand against such people? Allah stated: Do not be overawed by this spending carried out to block people from the path of truth. These pebbles and straws cannot block the flood that is coming. Indeed, they spent with liberality, and will spend further, but the only result of this spending will be regret; they will face defeat in this world, and in the Hereafter, they will be gathered to be cast into Hell. The preposition *ilā* (to) with *yuhsharūna* (will be gathered) adds the meaning of being driven. We have preserved this in the translation.

لِيَمِيْزَ اللّٰهُ الْخَبِيْثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ 

This describes the *furqān* that will appear in the Hereafter: on that Day, Allah will separate all the evil from the good, piling the evil one upon another, and then casting the entire pile into Hell. *Rakm* means piling things one upon another, as is done when burning rubbish: everything is gathered and piled, and then set on fire. The benefit of piling is that the fire burns with full intensity upon finding a large quantity of fuel, and every part of the pile helps burn the other. This points to the fact that just as the disbelievers supported one another in supporting disbelief in this world, they will serve as fuel for one another in Hell.

'Those are the losers.' He stated: The true losers are these. Because in this world, their end will be that their wealth will go to waste, bringing them regret, and they will face a humiliating defeat; and in the Hereafter, they will be gathered like a pile of rubbish to be cast into Hell. Losers of this world and the Hereafter.

قُلْ لِّلَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْٓا اِنْ يَّنْتَهُوْا يُغْفَرْ لَهُمْ مَّا قَدْ سَلَفَ 

As we pointed out above, this is an invitation to the Quraysh to seek forgiveness. The meaning is: if they wish to escape this punishment of which they are being warned, let them desist from this path. Let them repent, reform, and accept the Prophet's call. If they change their path, Allah will forgive the mischiefs and wrongdoings they committed in the past, and they will not be apprehended for them.

'but if they return [to war] - then the precedent of the former peoples has already passed.' This is a threat after the encouragement. The meaning is: if they do not desist, persisting in their mischiefs, let them remember that they will face the same law of God faced by previous nations who denied their messengers. This points to 'Ad, Thamud, Midian, and the people of Lot, whose history was detailed in Al-A'raf. We have clarified in many places that once a messenger is sent to a nation, the proof is completed against them. Therefore, if that nation persists in disbelief, Allah inevitably destroys them, whether through His physical punishment or through the swords of the believers. A messenger's sending represents the highest and final stage of completing the proof; therefore, no further respite is granted after it.

**Instruction to the Muslims to Fight:** 'And fight them until there is no fitnah and the religion, all of it, is for Allah.' This is the command to the Muslims to fight the Quraysh, and two targets are described for this fighting: first, that trial (*fitnah*) is eliminated; second, that the religion belongs entirely to Allah.

*Fitnah* is used in the sense of persecution—namely: that systematic violence the Quraysh and their allies used to block Muslims from the true religion. He stated: Fight them, and continue this fighting, until this trial is eradicated from this land, leaving no possibility for anyone to persecute a Muslim because of his embracing Islam, whether he is rich or poor, free or slave.

**Establishing the Religion of God in the Sanctuary:** 'and the religion, all of it, is for Allah'—meaning: on the land of the Sanctuary, no other religion remains except the religion of Allah. We explained above that the Kaaba was constructed as the center and Qiblah of Abraham's religion. This House was constructed for the worship of the One God, and its guardianship was designated for those who adhere to Abraham's religion and establish prayer. The Quraysh, as clarified above, destroyed all the purposes of this House, turning it into a temple of idols. When Allah, in accordance with Abraham's prayer, sent His final Prophet among this nation, and he invited them to the original religion of Abraham, the self-interested leaders of the Quraysh became enemies of the Prophet and his companions, blocking them from this House though they were its true guardians. Allah commanded the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims: Fight these usurpers and betrayers, and continue this fighting, until on the land of the Sanctuary, no other religion remains except the religion of Allah—Islam, which was the religion of Abraham. The Prophet emphasized this in the words: 'Two religions shall not coexist on this peninsula.' This ruling relates fundamentally to the land of the Sanctuary. But protecting it from the influences of disbelief and polytheism is impossible without keeping the entire region free from their interference. Furthermore, just as Mecca is sacred since Abraham's time, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) declared Medina sacred, which broadened the scope of this ruling. This made it necessary to exclude non-Muslim elements from the entire region containing the two Sanctuaries. Consequently, the dominance of the Quraysh was ended, and then the Jews and Christians were also gradually excluded from here. We confine ourselves to these indications here; detailed discussion on this will come in the commentary of Surah At-Tawbah and Surah Al-Hajj.

'But if they cease - then indeed, Allah is Seeing of what they do.' The meaning of 'cease' (*intahaw*) is the same as explained under the preceding verse: if they reform their path, Allah will not look at their past errors but will look at their future deeds; if they display sincerity, He will deal with them accordingly.

**The Promise of Help to the Muslims:** 'And if they turn away - then know that Allah is your protector. Excellent is the protector, and excellent is the helper.' And if they turn away, persisting in their obstinacy and hostility, then your Protector and Refuge is Allah. Do not fear their abundance of numbers and means. God will display His glory in the future just as He has displayed it up to now. He is the Excellent Protector; He never disappoints those who turn to Him in their difficulties. He is the Excellent Helper; those for whose help He rises are never defeated.

The upcoming subject (Verses 41 to 49): You will recall that this Surah began with a question regarding the spoils of war. There, after giving a fundamental answer, the discourse turned toward correcting the weaknesses of a group of Muslims revealed by this question and other similar matters. Now, in connection with that question, He described the rule for distributing the spoils, emphasizing to the Muslims: accept this distribution of Allah with cheerfulness, because whatever you obtained was obtained through God's design and carsazi, and whatever you obtain in the future will be obtained through His design. Do not assume that this is your achievement; the actual thing is God's design which He brings to fruition through you.

Afterward, He gave several instructions regarding future battles, which are indispensable for success and distinguish those who fight to elevate the word of Allah from other warriors. In this light, recite the verses, keeping in mind that the address throughout this Surah is directed fundamentally to that group of Muslims who were not yet fully refined. He stated:

وَاعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّـمَا غَنِمْتُمْ مِّنْ شَيْءٍ فَاَنَّ لِلّٰهِ خُمُسَهٗ وَلِلرَّسُوْلِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبٰي وَالْيَتٰمٰي وَالْمَسٰكِيْنِ وَالْمَسٰكِيْنِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيْلِ ۙ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ اٰمَنْتُمْ بِاللّٰهِ وَمَآ اَنْزَلْنَا عَلٰي عَبْدِنَا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقَانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعٰنِ ۭوَاللّٰهُ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيْرٌ 

**The Position of 'I'lamu' (And Know):** The word 'and know' (*wa-i'lamū*) in this context highlights the certainty and significance of the ruling described here. Since some people had raised improper questions regarding the spoils of war, as is known from the beginning of the Surah, He first commented on their weaknesses, and then began the answer to their question with a warning kalamat, so the people would listen with open ears and submit to it without any difference or dispute, as a decree from the King of the universe.

**The Status of the Spoils of War:** *Ghanima al-shay'* means: he obtained it without any exchange. From this is *ghanīmah* (spoils), which refers to the wealth and assets obtained in the battlefield from the disbelievers by the Muslim Mujahideen. By expressing the wealth obtained in the battlefield through the words *nafl* or *ghanīmah*, the Quran clarified that this is not a compensation for Jihad, but a secondary and additional benefit a Mujahid receives. The Mujahid performs a duty in the path of Allah, and his reward is preserved with Allah for his eternal life. As for these things he receives along the way, they are additions: if obtained, they are spoils; if not, he should neither desire them nor grieve. Through this concept, the Quran corrected the pagan concept in which the Arabs had been embroiled—as they considered the primary object of war to be the looted wealth, measuring its benefit and harm by it. This concept had some remnant within a group of Muslims, which appeared at Badr and was corrected by the Quran.

'And know that anything you obtain of spoils - then indeed, for Allah is one fifth of it and for the Messenger and for the near relatives and the orphans and the needy and the traveler.' This is the detail of the brief answer given in verse 1: 'Say, "The spoils of war belong to Allah and the Messenger."' We indicated there that by giving this brief answer—that spoils do not belong to individuals as was the custom in the times of ignorance, but are collective property—the discourse turned toward correcting the weaknesses displayed by a group. Now, He described the method of distributing this collective property.

**Distribution of the Spoils:** He stated: One-fifth of it belongs to Allah, the Messenger, the near relatives, the orphans, the needy, and the travelers. That is: the custom of ignorance—whereby whoever looted any wealth kept it—is ended. Now, all spoils will be gathered, and after taking out the one-fifth share of Allah and the Messenger, the remaining four-fifths will be distributed among the Mujahideen. It should be kept in mind that during the times of ignorance, the tribal chiefs took one-fourth of the looted wealth, which was called *mirbā'*, and this wealth went to their personal use. Islam kept the share of Allah and the Messenger as only one-fifth, and indeed, as detailed ahead, returned it entirely to the society for its collective well-being.

**The Share of Allah and its Target:** In the details of the one-fifth, first the share of Allah is mentioned. Obviously, Allah is independent of all things. The primary target of His share must be those tasks that are of the nature of elevating the word of Allah, establishing the religion, and protecting and defending the nation. The forms of these tasks can vary with times and circumstances, but keeping the target of elevating the word of Allah in view is necessary in every form.

**The Share of the Messenger and its Character:** The second share mentioned is the Messenger's. I repeatedly think that this share belongs to him not as a Prophet, but as the head of the Islamic state; at a time when every moment of his was spent in performing the duties of this office, it was necessary to designate his share in this wealth. This share was, in reality, the share of the head of the state, which transferred to his successor after his passing.

**The Share of the Near Relatives and its Character:** The third share mentioned is that of the near relatives (*dhī al-qurbā*). This refers to the near relatives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—specifically: those whose support was the Prophet's responsibility, whether this responsibility arose from custom and law or from that moral obligation which lies on every generous-hearted head of a family regarding the poor, needy, and disabled of his family. This is clear from the generality of the word *dhī al-qurbā*, and shows that this share of the near relatives was to continue after the Prophet's passing. If it were limited only to his lifetime, there would have been no reason to mention it separately.

**The Share of the Orphans and the Needy and its Character:** The fourth share is that of the orphans, the needy, and the travelers. While mentioning their share, He did not repeat the particle *li* which was repeated with Allah, the Messenger, and the near relatives, but mentioned them under the near relatives. This is to honor this class—showing that they are like the near relatives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Those who are familiar with the character of the Islamic system know that in a true Islamic state, the orphans and the needy have the status of the family of the head of the state. Just as the head of the state must care for his own family, he must care for the orphans, the needy, and the travelers. The conduct of Hazrat Abu Bakr and Hazrat Umar is an undeniable and immortal witness to this. Hazrat Umar stated: 'My share in the wealth of the state is only like the share of a guardian in the wealth of an orphan.' He also repeatedly expressed that the responsibility of every orphan, needy person, and traveler in the state lies directly on him. A state where orphans are pushed away, the needy sleep hungry, and the traveler has no one to care for him has no connection to Islam, no matter how much it claims to be Islamic.

**The Collective Target of the Wealth of the Poor:** Those who take the particle *li* as denoting individual ownership (*tamlik*), thereby preventing the expenditure of this wealth on the collective well-being of the poor, needy, orphans, and travelers, do not carry much weight from the perspective of Arabic grammar. The particle *li* in Arabic does not denote only ownership, but carries multiple meanings, including benefit and well-being. We discussed this in detail in a independent article. In our view, it is entirely permissible for the Islamic government to spend these funds on those tasks it deems beneficial for the collective well-being of the poor and needy; individual ownership is not necessary under all circumstances.

'if you should believe in Allah and that which We sent down upon Our Servant on the Day of Criterion - the day when the two armies met.' 'The Day of Criterion' (*Yawm al-Furqān*) refers to the Day of the Battle of Badr. He clarified this in the words: 'the day when the two armies met.' We pointed to the reason why the Battle of Badr was termed the Day of Criterion above. The Quraysh leaders had made this war the criterion: whoever won would be considered on the truth. Thus, their own chosen criterion decided between truth and falsehood. Furthermore, on that Day, divine support appeared in such different forms that everyone saw with their own eyes who was on the truth and on whose side God was. 'and that which We sent down upon Our Servant' refers to this divine support.

This segment emphasizes the preceding command. The meaning is: submit to this command of distributing the spoils without any dispute, if you believe in Allah and in that support We sent down upon Our Servant on the Day of Decision. Since the address is directed to those who raised questions regarding the spoils, He stated: 'if you should believe in Allah,' which reveals that such questions show that faith is not yet firmly rooted within you. It is also worth noting that He did not state: 'if you believe in that support We sent down upon you,' but stated: 'upon Our Servant,' which reveals that no group must assume that this victory was their achievement. This occurred through the carsazi of Allah and the blessing of His Messenger, to help whom Allah sent His army. Allah is competent over all things. Neither is Allah in need of anyone, nor is His Messenger dependent on anyone. Allah will send His victorious armies to help His Messenger whenever He wills.

اِذْ اَنْتُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الْقُصْوٰي وَالرَّكْبُ اَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ ۭوَلَوْ تَوَاعَدْتُّمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيْعٰدِ ۙ وَلٰكِنْ لِّيَقْضِيَ اللّٰهُ اَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُوْلًا لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْۢ بَيِّنَةٍ وَّيَحْيٰي مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْۢ بَيِّنَةٍ ۭ وَاِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَسَمِيْعٌ عَلِيْمٌ 

**Divine Carsazi in the Battle of Badr:** This describes an example of that divine design and carsazi pointed to in the preceding verse: how He brought you to the battlefield to face the enemy at the exact moment—you were at one end of the valley, the Quraysh were at the other, and the commercial caravan was passing below along the coast. We mentioned previously that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was shown in a dream the details of the arrival of the Quraysh army and the caravan. In accordance with the direction of this dream, you left Medina. And you arrived at Badr exactly at the moment when the Quraysh army reached the other end of the valley under the excuse of protecting their caravan. Having an army arrive at the battlefield to face the enemy at the exact moment is not a minor thing. The war depends largely on it. A slight delay can translate into defeat. Arriving too early is also not free from dangers and losses—at least, the issue of provisions was highly difficult for the Muslims, especially in that era when they were unarmed and poor. The two sides play many ruses in this regard, attempting to deceive each other. The designs are different from what is declared in the ultimatums. The target is different from what is pointed to. Allah protected the Muslims from all such ruses, bringing them under His own guidance to face the enemy at the exact place where they needed to be. He stated: This beautiful coincidence was designed by God. If you had made an appointment to meet, you would have differed on the appointment.

'but [it was] so that Allah might accomplish a matter already destined.' A verb is omitted before 'so that Allah might accomplish' (*liyaqdiya Allāhu*)—meaning: Allah brought you and your enemies to face each other in this manner specifically so that the matter decided in His scheme would be accomplished.

**The Benefits and Wisdom of God's Scheme:** 'that those who would perish might perish upon a clear evidence and those who would live might live upon a clear evidence.' This explains the purpose and wisdom of God's scheme. The meaning is: God intended that a clash occur between the Quraysh and the Muslims to display the *furqān* that would make the truth clear and the falsehood clear, so that after this, those who choose the path of destruction would do so after the proof was completed, and those who choose the path of life would do so after seeing a clear proof. 'Perishing' and 'living' refer to spiritual and moral death and life. The preposition *'an* (upon/from) in Arabic also points to the source and origin of a thing. The Battle of Badr placed such a clear proof before both the believers and the disbelievers that no excuse remained for the disbelievers, and no doubt remained for the believers. For the disbelievers, their disbelief was laid bare; and for the believers, their faith shone like the sun.

'And indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.' Referencing these attributes alongside this scheme is meant to show: where were you, where were the Quraysh, and where was the caravan, yet Allah knew all secrets, heard all whispers, and knew all designs, and then gathered everyone to accomplish what He decided, because He is Hearing and Knowing.

اِذْ يُرِيْكَهُمُ اللّٰهُ فِيْ مَنَامِكَ قَلِيْلًا ۭوَلَوْ اَرٰىكَهُمْ كَثِيْرًا لَّفَشِلْتُمْ وَلَتَنَازَعْتُمْ فِي الْاَمْرِ وَلٰكِنَّ اللّٰهَ سَلَّمَ ۭ اِنَّهٗ عَلِيْمٌۢ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُوْرِ 

**Showing the Situation to the Prophet in a Dream:** This describes the second example of Allah's carsazi: He showed the disbelievers' army to the Prophet in a dream as few, and the Prophet mentioned them to the Muslims as a small group, and indeed, as passed in verse 7, gave the glad tiding that this small group would be defeated by the Muslims. Allah did this because if He had shown them as numerous—as they actually were—the Prophet would have mentioned them to the Muslims as such, which would have made the weaker Muslims lose heart and differ on whether to fight or not. Since Allah is aware of the weaknesses of the hearts, He chose this design so the Muslims' courage would remain intact, and they would be protected from displaying any weakness.

**Refuting a Wrong Concept:** Some have deduced from this that sometimes, even prophets are shown things contrary to reality in dreams. Thus, they claim that the Quraysh army was massive, but shown to the Prophet as few. In our view, this is incorrect. A dream is among the means of revelation for a prophet; therefore, a prophet's dream cannot be contrary to reality. However, sometimes, a reality appears in a dream under a metaphorical garb, or the spiritual and moral reality of a thing is shown instead of its outward form. This was the case here. The disbelievers' army, though massive in number, was highly weak and valueless in spirit and morals. This spiritual smallness was shown in the dream as smallness in number. It is this aspect of dreams because of which the need for interpretation arises, and in whose interpretation even a prophet can sometimes face a temporary reflection. But it is completely wrong that a prophet's dream is ever contrary to reality.

It should also be kept in mind that the Quran used the word 'few' (*qalīl*) in a highly jucha-tula manner. In Arabic, the word *qalīl* is used not only for smallness in number and quantity, but also for what is valueless and low in status. A poet of Hamasah wrote: 

*If I am few in the eyes of your wicked,*
*it is no grief, for I am numerous in the eyes of your good.*

We confine ourselves to this indication here. We will discuss dreams in detail under the relevant context.

**A Specific Character of the Address:** Although the address in 'you would have lost courage' (*lafashiltum*) and 'argued' (*wa-latanāza'tum*) is general, it does not imply that it relates to the entire congregation of Muslims, but refers to that specific group of weaker Muslims whose conduct has been under discussion since the beginning of the Surah. In verse 7, their state was described: despite knowing that the Prophet was exiting to face the Quraysh army, they attempted to turn him toward the caravan, and despite the Prophet's glad tiding that they would win, they trembled as if being driven to death. The discourse here is directed to them. For those whose courage was such, if they had heard beforehand that the face-off was with a massive army, their hearts would have sunk. Out of regard for them, Allah showed the moral reality of the enemy's army rather than its numerical size.

Note also that the verbs 'He showed you' (*yurīka*), 'your dream' (*manāmika*), and 'He showed them to you' (*arākahum*) are addressed to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), because what was shown in the dream was shown to him. The Prophet is like the heart and eye of the believers; therefore, though he is the one who sees, what he sees relates to all. For this reason, while seeing is restricted to the Prophet, the description of the reaction includes all Muslims.

وَاِذْ يُرِيْكُمُوْهُمْ اِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِيْٓ اَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيْلًا وَّ يُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِيْٓ اَعْيُنِهِمْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللّٰهُ اَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُوْلًا ۭوَاِلَى اللّٰهِ تُرْجَعُ الْاُمُوْرُ 

**Another Carsazi of Allah:** This describes another of His designs: when the two armies faced each other, He showed the disbelievers to the Muslims as few, and showed the Muslims to the disbelievers as few in the initial stage, so neither side would fear taking the clash, and the battle decided in Allah's scheme would be accomplished.

The reason why the Muslims appeared few to the disbelievers was that their outward-looking eyes saw only the Muslims' numerical size, while their spiritual and moral strength remained hidden from them. Yet, this same small army, as we explained elsewhere, appeared to them as a massive storm during the battle, because at that stage, the Muslims' spiritual and moral reality was fully displayed.

**A Psychological Reality of Battle:** The reason why the disbelievers appeared few to the Muslims despite their numerical majority was that Allah laid bare their spiritual and moral reality before the Muslims. The eye is largely subject to the heart. If the heart possesses courage, enthusiasm, faith, and trust—the resolve to die for a truthful purpose being sincere—even a mountain appears like a heap of sand; but if the heart is empty of these, a person takes a squirrel for a mountain and a goat for a lion. Nothing is stronger than man, and nothing is weaker; and both this strength and weakness have their source not in the outward but in his inner self. In this material age, it is not easy to make people believe that one hundred Muslims could consider themselves heavier than one thousand disbelievers, but this is a reality to which history is witness, and anyone can experience it today if they become familiar with the sweetness of faith.

'And to Allah all matters are returned' points to the reality that the reins of all matters are in the hand of Allah. Showing as few or numerous, winning or losing, increasing or decreasing—everything is fundamentally from Allah. Man's duty is to perform his part, leaving everything else to God, never falling into the misunderstanding that he can accomplish anything independent of God.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اِذَا لَقِيْتُمْ فِئَةً فَاثْبُتُوْا وَاذْكُرُوا اللّٰهَ كَثِيْرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُوْنَ 

**Instructions to the Believers Regarding War:** This gives instructions for future stages: when you face a group of the disbelievers, remain steadfast and remember Allah much, so you may succeed. Steadfastness is necessary because Allah's help always appears from behind the veil of means. This world is a place of testing for the servants. When they display their capacity, Allah's help appears through it. The servants have no right to test God like the Children of Israel did—remaining in their homes while expecting God to conquer the city and hand them the keys, so they could enter.

**Steadfastness is Achieved Through Remembering God:** Remembering God is the means to steadfastness. We mentioned above that the primary strength is the strength of the heart, and the heart receives strength from faith. Here, He clarified that faith remains green and lush through the rain of remembering God. While this remembrance of God is necessary at all times for the life of faith, when circumstances are testing, it is required in greater measure, which is why the restriction 'much' (*kathīran*) is placed here.

**The Means to Success:** The word 'success' (*falāh*) is a comprehensive word, comprising success in both this world and the Hereafter. A mere victory can be attained without remembering God, but it cannot be the means to success. Success is achieved through that victory whose door is opened with the help of remembering God, and in which the victors possess the companionship of God. It should be kept in mind that in an army's steadfastness, the primary factor is always its courage. The significance of this was recognized in the past, and is recognized today. Islam has shown that the means to preserve courage for those striving in the cause of Allah is remembering God, and indeed, this is the source of courage and resolve within a believer.

**A Specific Meaning of Obeying Allah and His Messenger:** 'And obey Allah and His Messenger...' Here, alongside the general meaning of obeying Allah and His Messenger, a specific meaning is also in view—namely: display complete organization and discipline before the enemy. Obey the commands Allah gave, and obey every command the Prophet gives without question. Just as the heart is scattered when empty of remembering God, the organization of the congregation is ruined when obedience is loose, and then its power is lost.

'and do not dispute and [thus] lose courage and then your strength depart.' This is stated through a negative aspect: do not dispute regarding the commands given by Allah and His Messenger, otherwise division will arise within the congregation, which will inevitably make you lose courage and lose your power. This is the instruction a group violated during the Battle of Uhud, bringing a bitter test upon the entire congregation. The Quran referred to this in Ali 'Imran: 'until when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [given by the Prophet] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love.' (Ali 'Imran: 152).

'and be patient. Indeed, Allah is with the patient.' What was stated in 'remain steadfast' above is stated here in 'be patient'. However, the former was addressed to individuals, while this is addressed to the congregation. The meaning is: if you desire God's help, support, and companionship, prove yourselves worthy of it through your collective character. God does not support a scattered herd, but those who stand as a solid structure in His path.

وَلَا تَكُوْنُوْا كَالَّذِيْنَ خَرَجُوْا مِنْ دِيَارِهِمْ بَطَرًا وَّرِئَاۗءَ النَّاسِ وَيَصُدُّوْنَ عَنْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ 

**The Meaning of 'Batar':** *Batar* means turning away from the truth with pride and arrogance. The Quran used it to express that insolence and rebellion which overcomes a person or group when they receive Allah's blessings, causing them to choose pride and transgression instead of gratitude and humility. In Surah Al-Qasas: 'And how many a city have We destroyed that exulted (*batarat*) in its livelihood.'

This is a warning to the Muslims against adopting a path contrary to the one commanded in 'remember Allah much' above. 'like those who' (*ka-alladhīna*) points to the Quraysh, who, as we indicated, left for Badr in the pride of their numbers, weapons, and means, with great pomp and show. He stated: Do not adopt their path at any stage. You have risen to establish the worship and obedience of God in the world; therefore, the humility of worship and servitude must be visible upon you at all times, whether in peace or war. Although the purpose here is to tell the Muslims that their war is not a war but a worship of God, whose character of worship must be visible, it also reveals that Muslims will face situations in the future where, if not alert, they could fall into such trials. This is like a glad tiding, alongside a warning: always remain attached to God, and do not exult when abundance of means is obtained. The actual thing is not the means, but the carsazi and companionship of God.

It should be remembered that just as a group violated the instruction of 'do not dispute,' bringing a severe test upon the congregation at Uhud, the reliance on numbers fell the Muslims into a severe test at Hunayn, to which the Quran pointed: 'and on the Day of Hunayn when your great number pleased you, but it availed you not at all, and the earth was vast as it is, was straitened for you, then you turned back in flight.'

'showing off to the people' points to that desire for show which possessed every leader at Badr. Its contrary is sincerity for Allah. The meaning is: whatever you do, do it always for Allah, because you do not seek your reward from this world, but from your Lord in the Hereafter.

'and averting [people] from the path of Allah. And Allah is encompassing of what they do.' That is: all their show and enthusiasm was to block the servants of Allah from His path. These foolish ones do not know that no matter how broad a person spreads his net and displays his speed, everything of his is in the hand of God. God has placed a fence around all his movements.

وَاِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطٰنُ اَعْمَالَهُمْ وَقَالَ لَا غَالِبَ لَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَاِنِّىْ جَارٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ فَلَمَّا تَرَاۗءَتِ الْفِئَتٰنِ نَكَصَ عَلٰي عَقِبَيْهِ وَقَالَ اِنِّىْ بَرِيْۗءٌ مِّنْكُمْ اِنِّىْٓ اَرٰي مَا لَا تَرَوْنَ اِنِّىْٓ اَخَافُ اللّٰهَ ۭوَاللّٰهُ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ 

That is: while the abundance of numbers and means played a role in the Quraysh's display of pride and show, Satan—who has been granted respite specifically to mislead people from the path of God—also whispered to them: 'Forward, who can stand against you today? I am your protector and helper.' He supported them until the two armies faced each other, but then he withdrew, saying: 'I am disassociated from you; indeed, I see what you do not see; indeed, I fear Allah.'

**The Jews' Plots in the Battle of Badr:** We explained elsewhere that devils (*shayātīn*) are from both the jinn and humans. Here, my mind repeatedly thinks that this indicates the Jews. Both the books of history and biography and the Quranic indications show that the Jews were fearful of the Prophet's call from the very beginning. When he emigrated to Medina and received the support of the Ansar, they felt a heavy stone was placed on their chest. Aside from political anxieties, they feared that he might indeed be the Prophet prophesied in their scriptures. They were not ready to accept any prophethood outside their nation. But due to their cowardice, they did not muster the courage to take any direct action against him. However, they secretly incited the Quraysh leaders, and also conspired within the Aws and Khazraj in Medina. Under such circumstances, it is highly probable that when the Quraysh made the plan to attack Medina under the excuse of protecting their caravan, the Jews' advice was involved, and they incited the Quraysh: your massive army is enough to destroy the Muslims, but if needed, we are also ready to help. Although the Jews were partners in a treaty with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), ahead under verses 56-57, it will be clear that they did not respect it, but were always engaged in plots. However, due to their cowardice, they never had the courage to come forward. On this occasion, too, they incited the Quraysh, but when the two sides faced each other and they saw the Muslims' resolve, they sat down. On this occasion, that fear they harbored regarding the Prophet's mission must have appeared—as they had heard from their scriptures that he would be accompanied by armies of angels. Also remember that the Battle of Badr, in its plan, numbers, and purpose, was similar to the war fought by the Children of Israel under Saul against Goliath.

**The Deceits of the Hypocrites:** The metaphor the Quran used here for the Jews was also used for those hypocrites who belonged to the Jews and conspired with them while entering the ranks of the Muslims. They assured them that if the Muslims took any step against them, they would support them, but the Quran clarified that this is a deceit similar to the deceit Satan plays on those who rely on him. Since these two metaphors are highly similar, we quote the metaphor here to clarify the one under discussion. In Surah Al-Hashr, this character of the hypocrites is described:

'Have you not considered those who practice hypocrisy, saying to their brothers who have disbelieved among the People of the Book, "If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will not obey, in regard to you, anyone - ever; and if you are fought, we will surely aid you."? But Allah testifies that they are liars. If they are expelled, they will not leave with them, and if they are fought, they will not aid them. And [even] if they should aid them, they will surely turn their backs; then they will not be aided.' (Al-Hashr: 11-12).

Then, He gave their metaphor in these words:

'[The hypocrites are] like the example of Satan when he says to man, "Disbelieve." But when he disbelieves, he says, "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds." So the outcome for both of them is that they will be in the Fire, abiding eternally therein. And that is the recompense of the wrongdoers.' (Al-Hashr: 16-17).

Just as He compared the Jewish hypocrites to Satan there, in the verse under discussion, although the word 'Satan' (*al-shaytān*) is used, it points to the Jews. The metaphorical style was chosen because these mischiefs of the Jews were still behind the curtain; therefore, the Quran chose to keep them behind the curtain, but lifted some corners of the veil through indications, so the Jews would know that Allah is not ignorant of their deeds, and the Muslims would remain alert. Pay special attention to the words: 'no master of victory is there for you today among the people, and indeed, I am your protector,' 'indeed, I see what you do not see,' and 'indeed, I fear Allah.'

**Expressing the Jews' Inner State:** Stating that Satan incites man to disbelieve and then withdraws, saying 'I am disassociated from you, indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds,' is an expression of his conduct and inner state; Satan does not say this with his tongue to the one who disbelieves. Similarly, here, stating of the Jews 'he said, "I am disassociated from you; indeed, I see what you do not see; indeed, I fear Allah"' is an expression of their conduct and mind; they did not say this with words to the Quraysh. The Quran has used the verb 'to say' (*qāla*) for what a person says within his heart in many places. Out of jealousy, the Jews desired that the Quraysh destroy Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions, but they harbored the fear in their hearts that he was indeed the Messenger of Allah, and clashing with him was clashing with a mountain; therefore, they were not ready to come forward themselves. They wanted someone else to bear this risk.

This also clarified that inciting others to a crime while avoiding joining them out of fear of falling into trouble is 'satanic piety'. The Quran clarified that those who support others in crime but do not join them out of fear of God's apprehension will not be saved from His punishment; rather, just as those who enter the field of crime will be cast into Hell, those who sit by the side to instruct them will also be cast into Hell, even if they did not enter out of 'fear of God'. They are like the one who teaches others to steal and commit crimes, but does not join them out of fear of the police. Obviously, no law forgives such people; rather, when apprehended, they receive a heavier punishment. This is what the Quran stated in 'So the outcome for both of them is that they will be in the Fire, abiding eternally therein' in Al-Hashr, which shows that the Jews were also in this satanic piety regarding the Muslims. They desired that the Muslims be destroyed, and incited the Quraysh for this, but did not join them in the battlefield because they feared that divine army and police pointed to in 'indeed, I see what you do not see.'

اِذْ يَقُوْلُ الْمُنٰفِقُوْنَ وَالَّذِيْنَ فِيْ قُلُوْبِهِمْ مَّرَضٌ غَرَّ هٰٓؤُلَاءِ دِيْنُهُمْ 

We explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah that when the word 'disease' (*marad*) is used alongside hypocrisy, it refers to jealousy. After the Jews' plots, He mentioned the hypocrites' and hater's attempts to discourage: on this occasion, to discourage the Muslims, they began saying: 'These people have been deluded by their religion.' They are few, yet have rose to face the massive army of the Quraysh; they are going to eat a grain of wheat from an elephant. The delusion of religion has deprived them of their senses. Such statements and taunts, especially from within, could have had dangerous effects when the Muslims were unarmed and few, but Allah protected them from their poison.

'But whoever relies upon Allah - then indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.' This means that those who relied on Allah and rose, in opposition to these hypocrites and haters, saw with their own eyes that God is Dominant and Wise. He supports those who rely on Him; His power cannot be defeated, He designs for them, and no one's plan succeeds against His design.

The upcoming subject (Verses 50 to 58): Next, He clarified that this defeat they suffered is not the end. The actual punishment is to face them when they die; this defeat is merely a warning. The law of Allah is that He does not deprive a nation of His blessings unless they corrupt their own path. When a nation corrupts its path, He first warns them. If they do not learn a lesson from the warning, persisting in their rebellion, His decisive punishment falls upon them. The treatment meted out to the people of Pharaoh is an example: first He apprehended them with various trials to open their eyes, but when they did not, He drowned them.

Afterward, He clarified: The worst of creatures before God are those who persist in disbelief, having no faith, and who violate their treaties with you repeatedly. If you face them in war, strike them in a manner that deters those behind them, and if you fear betrayal from any group, throw their treaty back to them. God does not like the betrayers. Recite the verses:

وَلَوْ تَرٰٓي اِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوا ۙ الْمَلٰۗىِٕكَةُ يَضْرِبُوْنَ وُجُوْهَهُمْ وَاَدْبَارَهُمْ ۚ وَذُوْقُوْا عَذَابَ الْحَرِيْقِ ۔ ذٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ اَيْدِيْكُمْ وَاَنَّ اللّٰهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامِ لِّلْعَبِيْدِ 

**An Omission of the Response:** In 'And if you could but see...', the response to the condition (*jawāb al-shart*) is omitted according to the rule of Arabic grammar, which is highly eloquent in such contexts. This reveals that the scene is of an indescribable horror.

**A Specific Style of Negating:** In 'is not ever unjust to [His] servants' (*laysa bi-thallāmin lil-'abīd*), there is a specific style of the Arabic language: when negation is placed on an intensive form, it negates the action entirely—meaning: God is not unjust to His servants in the least. Imru' al-Qays used similar structures like *laysa bi-fa''āl* and *laysa bi-qattāl*—meaning: so-and-so has no capacity to act or fight, being a champion of words, not deeds.

The meaning is: the defeat the disbelievers suffered at Badr is minor; the actual punishment is what falls upon them at the hand of the angels at the time of their death. If you could see it, you would estimate what a plight is to face them. After that is the punishment of Hell, of which no concept can be formed today.

**Everyone Will Face Their Own Deeds:** 'That is for what your hands have sent ahead and [because] Allah is not ever unjust to His servants.' That is: they will face their own deeds. The bitter harvest they sowed, watered, and grew in the world will be placed before them. Allah will commit no injustice against them. He does not tolerate even a particle of injustice against His servants. His law and justice are impartial. Everyone will reap what they sowed.

كَدَاْبِ اٰلِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَالَّذِيْنَ مِنْ قَبْلِهمْ ۭكَفَرُوْا بِاٰيٰتِ اللّٰهِ فَاَخَذَهُمُ اللّٰهُ بِذُنُوْبِهِمْ ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ قَوِيٌّ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ 

**The Meaning of 'Al':** We explained elsewhere that 'family' (*āl*) does not apply only to children, but to the nation and followers as well.

Now, He stated: This calamity that befell the Quraysh is similar to what befell the people of Pharaoh and previous nations—they showed ingratitude for Allah's blessings, disregarded His signs, and denied His verses, so Allah apprehended them for their sins, because God is strong and impartial in executing His law of recompense.

**Punishment as a Warning:** The context proves that 'Allah apprehended them for their sins' (*fa-akhadhahumu Allāhu bi-dhunūbihim*) points to those apprehensions through which He warns nations. During the sending of a messenger, such warnings appear frequently. For details of this divine law, see Al-A'raf 94, Al-An'am 42, and Al-Baqarah 155. The meaning is: this calamity that befell the Quraysh is meant to shake and wake them. This occurred in accordance with the law of God, and its examples have passed in the history of previous nations. Now, it remains to be seen whether the Quraysh benefit from it, or persist in denying the Prophet's call in imitation of previous nations. If they persist, Allah will destroy them just as He destroyed the people of Pharaoh and other nations. Here, 'Like the custom of the people of Pharaoh' (*kada'bi āli fir'awna*) is repeated twice; this is not a redundancy, but states two different points. First, He stated that this is a warning of the same nature as the warning given to Pharaoh and other nations. Second, He stated that if they do not benefit from this warning, His decisive punishment will fall upon them just as it fell upon those nations.

ذٰلِكَ بِاَنَّ اللّٰهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً اَنْعَمَهَا عَلٰي قَوْمٍ حَتّٰي يُغَيِّرُوْا مَا بِاَنْفُسِهِمْ 

This explains the wisdom behind why He arranged to warn and remind nations. He stated: When Allah bestows a blessing upon a nation, He does not do so arbitrarily; rather, it is based on certain qualities and character. Similarly, when He deprives a nation of His blessing, He does not do so arbitrarily, but when He sees that the nation has deprived itself of those qualities and character by virtue of which they deserved that blessing. This made it necessary that when a blessed nation falls into corruption, God warns and reminds them to complete the proof. If they wake through this warning, their entitlement remains. If they do not, they are deprived of His blessing after the proof is completed, and the responsibility lies on them; God commits no injustice against them. This is a warning to the Quraysh: opportunity still remains; benefit from it. God does not wish to seize the blessing He bestowed upon you, but your own corruption of beliefs and deeds has caused it. You can restore your entitlement by changing yourselves and reforming.

'and that Allah is Hearing and Knowing.' That is: God's dealing in deposing or establishing nations is not like a blind man's staff, but is based entirely on His hearing and knowing. He grants power, authority, and means to nations, while observing, hearing, and knowing what they are doing and where they are heading, and His dealing with them is based on this impartial hearing and knowing.

كَدَاْبِ اٰلِ فِرْعَوْنَ 

This describes the fate that will face the Quraysh if they do not learn a lesson from these warnings—namely: they will face God's decisive punishment instead of warnings. Note the difference in words and tone between this verse and verse 52: in verse 52, it was 'they disbelieved in the signs of Allah' (*kafarū bi-āyāti Allāhi*); here, it is 'they denied the signs of their Lord' (*kadh-dhabū bi-āyāti rabbihim*). There, it was 'Allah apprehended them' (*fa-akhadhahumu Allāhu*); here, it is 'We destroyed them for their sins and We drowned the people of Pharaoh' (*fa-ahlaknāhum bi-dhunūbihim wa-aghraqnā āla fir'awna*). Without keeping this difference in view, the context of the two verses cannot be clear.

اِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَاۗبِّ عِنْدَ اللّٰهِ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُوْنَ 

**The Jews' Breach of Treaties:** Now, He gave instructions regarding the Jews and those tribes with whom the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had made treaties of non-neutrality after emigrating to Medina—such as the Juhaynah, Banu Damrah, and Banu Mudlij. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) respected his treaties, but the Jews did not respect them for even a single day, and these tribes, under the incitement of the Jews or the pressure of the Quraysh, continually violated the treaties, secretly or openly.

**The Meaning of 'Sharru al-Dawab' (The Worst of Beasts):** The intensity in 'the worst of beasts' (*sharra al-dawābb*) was explained under verse 22. This style of expression negates the justification for their existence and survival. The meaning is: the value of any human group exists only as long as some capacity to think and understand remains within them. When this capacity is lost, so that they neither believed in the past nor will believe in the future, they are merely a pile of rubbish. There is no benefit in their remaining on God's earth. It should be kept in mind that a messenger represents the final stage of completing the proof. Those whose blindness remains after the messenger are past cure, and their remaining on the earth is entirely empty of purpose. The respite Allah grants anyone to live is not merely to live, but to think, understand, and reform their lives.

اَلَّذِيْنَ عٰهَدْتَّ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنْقُضُوْنَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِيْ كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَتَّقُوْنَ 

**The Moral Lowliness of the Mentioned Group:** This describes their state. 'Among them' (*minhum*)—meaning: from those who disbelieved. We pointed to this previously: they made treaties, but did not respect them for even a single day. Whenever a test arose, they did not refrain from harming the Muslims. Their sympathies were with the Quraysh. Their repeated breach of treaties after making them was proof of their hard-heartedness, lowliness of character, and lack of honor. 'Every time' (*fī kulli marrah*) means: whenever a situation arose for which the treaty had been made, they did not respect the treaty but violated it. 'They do not fear' (*lā yattaqūna*) means: they do not fear the consequences of breach of treaty, though respecting and honoring treaties is recognized in human customs and its questioning will be asked before God.

فَاِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِمْ مَّنْ خَلْفَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُوْنَ 

*Thaqifa* means to find, and *tashrīd* means to scatter or disperse. The meaning is: currently, whatever they are doing, they are doing behind the curtain. None of them has the courage to come forward. If any of them muster the courage to come forward and face you in battle, strike them in such a manner that their joints are shattered, and those who are behind them, preparing to rise, also lose their wings.

'that perhaps they may remember'—meaning: seeing their end, they also learn a lesson that if they commit similar mischief, they will face the same end.

وَاِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِنْ قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً فَانْۢبِذْ اِلَيْهِمْ عَلٰي سَوَاۗءٍ 

This gave a general instruction regarding all such treaties: your responsibility is not one-sided; it exists only as long as the other side also respects it. If they do not, throw their treaty back to them. 'In an equal manner' (*'alā sawā'*) means: you are authorized to take a step equal to theirs. This shows that the response to a brick must not be a stone, but must be of equal weight. Some have deduced from this that it is necessary to inform the other party of the termination of the treaty. I do not see any proof for this in these words. Nonetheless, it is derived that a mere hypothetical fear is not sufficient to declare a treaty void, but some practical violation of theirs must have appeared. First, the verb *takhāfanna* is emphasized. Second, the restriction *'alā sawā'* also highlights this.

'Indeed, Allah does not like the betrayers.' This is a declaration of disassociation—meaning: Allah has no connection with those who break their treaties. And when Allah has no connection with them, how can the believers befriend such people? This also indicates that if the believers maintain ties with them, they will be maintaining ties with those whom God dislikes.

The upcoming subject (Verses 59 to 66): Next, He instructed the Muslims to remain prepared for Jihad, gathering weapons and strength for it. This is because the defeat the Quraysh suffered at Badr kindled a fire within them, and the Jews—who had expected the Quraysh to destroy the Muslims—also began making new plans upon their failure. To face these developments, Muslims were instructed: prepare for Jihad with full enthusiasm.

In this connection, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was assured: if these opponents express any inclination toward peace, do not hesitate to accept it. If they attempt to play a trick under the cover of this peace, then God—who helped you with His support and these few Muslims at Badr—is still with you.

Then, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was consoled: do not fear the small number of your companions. The support of Allah and the companionship of these few Muslims is sufficient for you. These drops will turn into a flood. Assure the Muslims that ten of them will be heavier than one hundred of the disbelievers. True strength is the strength of the hearts, not of numbers. Those who face you are merely empty of heart.

The final verse of this group, verse 66, was revealed, as is clear from its context, long after this, when the number of Muslims had increased; since its content belonged to this subject, it was placed here. It lessened some of the responsibility laid upon the Muslims in the preceding verse. Ahead, its explanation is coming.

'And let not those who disbelieve think they have escaped. Indeed, they will not cause failure [to Allah].' This emphasizes what was stated above: that He encompasses all their movements and designs. He stated: We have begun our pursuit of them. Let them remove this misunderstanding that they can escape Us. They cannot escape Our power. *A'jaza al-sayd* means: the prey escaped, and could not be caught.

وَاَعِدُّوْا لَهُمْ مَّاسْـتَـطَعْتُمْ مِّنْ قُوَّةٍ وَّمِنْ رِّبَاطِ الْخَيْلِ 

As is clear from Surah At-Tawbah 69, Hud 65, Al-Kahf 15, Fussilat, and other verses, the word 'strength' (*quwwah*) is also used for numerical strength and manpower.

*Ribāt al-khayl* refers to horses trained specifically for war, kept prepared for this purpose. Not all horses are useful in war. In the war of that era, horses possessed the primary significance, and based on the climate of Arabia, they had a special care for training horses. Muslims are instructed here: organize those capable of fighting, and prepare trained horses. Up to then, whenever an expedition arose, every soldier went out with whatever means he possessed; but in this verse, Muslims are instructed to increase their military power, both in terms of manpower and weapons of war. In the war of that era, horses possessed the same significance that tanks and fighter jets possess in this era. During the Battle of Badr, the Muslims had very few horses. For future stages, they are emphasized to increase their number.

'to terrify thereby the enemy of Allah and your enemy.' This explains the purpose of this preparation: that your awe and terror remain established in the hearts of the enemies of Allah and your enemies, so they do not dare attack you, taking you as a soft target. Here, all enemies of the Muslims are termed the enemies of Allah, because the Muslims' war was solely for the religion of Allah, empty of any other concern.

'and others besides them whom you do not know [but] whom Allah knows.' These enemies of Allah and the Muslims were of two kinds: those who had come forward openly—such as the Quraysh, who had been enemies since the first day; and those who were still behind the curtain—such as the Jews, whose secret plots were mentioned above, and those tribes with whom the Prophet had treaties of non-neutrality, but who had begun to prepare under the incitement of the Jews and the pressure of the Quraysh. Aside from them, there were those hypocrites who were highly skilled in hypocrisy, conspiring with the enemies. The Quran pointed to them in Surah At-Tawbah:

'And among those around you of the bedouins are hypocrites, and [also] from the people of Medina. They have become accustomed to hypocrisy. You do not know them, [but] We know them. We will punish them twice [in this world]; then they will be returned to a great punishment.' (At-Tawbah: 101).

Furthermore, there were external powers—such as the Romans, Ghassanids, and Persians—who came forward later when Islam had subdued the entire peninsula. While giving instructions for the present, the Quran pointed to those enemies hidden behind the curtains of the near or distant future, so the Muslims would look far and plan. Let them not assume that this was a temporary wave which came and has now passed.

'And whatever you spend in the cause of Allah will be fully repaid to you, and you will not be wronged.' This encourages spending for military preparations: whatever you spend for this purpose will not go to waste. Your every penny's account is present with Allah, and it will be fully repaid to you, without the slightest reduction. It should be kept in mind that this 'repaying' will occur in accordance with that principle which Allah has established for rewarding good deeds, as mentioned elsewhere in the the Quran.

وَاِنْ جَنَحُوْا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَهَا وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَي اللّٰهِ ۭاِنَّهٗ هُوَ السَّمِيْعُ الْعَلِيْمُ 

*Silm* or *salm* means peace and reconciliation, and it is also used as a feminine noun.

**Respecting the Collective Principle of Justice:** The command to fight given above is with the clarification 'until there is no fitnah and the religion, all of it, is for Allah,' which means that this war with the Quraysh is not to end until trial is eliminated and every trace of disbelief and polytheism is eradicated from the Sanctuary. Here, He clarified that this command is not contrary to accepting peace if the Quraysh desire it at any stage. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was permitted to accept their offer of peace. Based on the hostility the Quraysh leaders had displayed up to then, it was not expected that they would make any offer of peace with good intention; rather, there was a fear that they would do so only to commit mischief and play a trick, which is why trusting them was highly difficult. However, since the collective principle of justice demanded that the offer of peace must not be rejected, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed: accept the peace despite this fear, and place your trust in Allah. Allah is Hearing and Knowing. If you take this risk for a good purpose while relying on Him, He will help you, and the opponent will not succeed in taking any wrong advantage.

وَاِنْ يُّرِيْدُوْٓا اَنْ يَّخْدَعُوْكَ فَاِنَّ حَسْبَكَ اللّٰهُ 

That is: the maximum they can do is to attempt to deceive you; so do not worry about it, Allah is sufficient for you. Within 'Allah is sufficient for you' lies what is beyond the reach of the pen to describe, but is clear even without expression. What is worth reflecting on here is that the principle of truth and justice Islam commands for individual life is the same it commands for collective life, and indeed for international life—clarifying that even if some risk and fear exists, an offer of peace must not be rejected, but accepted while placing one's trust in Allah. This trust in Allah (*tawakkul*) should noted here: it is not limited to the mosque, but is the power of the believers in the battlefield and their support in international affairs.

'He it is who supported you with His help and with the believers.' This is the proof for 'Allah is sufficient for you': the God who helped you with His army of angels at Badr and crushed the massive army of the Quraysh through a handful of Muslims is the God who will help you when these opponents plot against you under the cover of peace, attempting to exploit your goodness.

وَاَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوْبِهِمْ ۭ لَوْ اَنْفَقْتَ مَا فِي الْاَرْضِ جَمِيْعًا مَّآ اَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوْبِهِمْ 

**A God-Designed Matter:** 'And brought together their hearts. If you had spent all that is in the earth, you could not have brought their hearts together.' This explains what was pointed to in 'supported you... with the believers': that this was no ordinary thing, but was a miracle of divine support. Gathering a crowd for some satanic purpose is not difficult; any demagogue can do it. But gathering a congregation of devoted companions solely for the work of God—where nothing is sought except His pleasure and the Hereafter—is impossible without the help, facilitation, and guidance of Allah. Those who gathered around the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) were, before adopting this new life, embroiled in all the evils of the times of ignorance. Their tribes were separate, and they harbored severe prejudices; their deities were separate, and they worshiped them blindly. Their interests were in conflict, and they used all means—lawful and unlawful, just and unjust—to achieve them. Turning such people away from all their prejudices, interests, customs, and habits, to forge them into an entirely new mold, and making this mold so beloved to them that they would leave their nation, home, family, property, and children for its sake—this is possible only for God. No human can accomplish this, even if he spent all the wealth of the earth on it. 'Indeed, He is Exalted in Might and Wise.' Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise; He accomplishes whatever He intends, and His every action is based on wisdom. This points to that law of guidance and misguidance which has been explained in multiple places.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللّٰهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ 

This verse serves as an introduction to the command given in the subsequent verse to encourage the Muslims to fight. The meaning is: the help of Allah and the companionship of these few Muslims is sufficient for you, so do not worry about the disbelievers' numbers and your companions' small number. Thus, what was stated in 'Allah is sufficient for you; He it is who supported you with His help and with the believers' is stated here in another style. Some have assumed that coordinating 'those who follow you' (*man ittaba'aka*) with Allah is improper, but this doubt arises from failing to reflect on the context of the discourse. Our interpretation is completely clear, in line with Quranic parallels, and free from any trace of impropriety.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا النَّبِيُّ حَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ عَلَي الْقِتَالِ ۭاِنَّ يَّكُنْ مِّنْكُمْ عِشْرُوْنَ صٰبِرُوْنَ يَغْلِبُوْا مِائَـتَيْنِ ۚ وَاِنْ يَّكُنْ مِّنْكُمْ مِّائَةٌ يَّغْلِبُوْٓا اَلْفًا مِّنَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا بِاَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُوْنَ
