**The Proportion of Strength Between Muslims and Disbelievers:** This explains the preceding statement: although your companions are few in number, they are great in quality. Twenty of your steadfast Muslims will overcome two hundred of the disbelievers, and one hundred of your Muslims will overcome one thousand of them. This is because He has blessed your companions with faithful insight, while your opponents are devoid of this insight.

**The Muslims' True Strength:** In 'because they are a people who do not understand' (*bi-annahum qawmun lā yafqahūna*), 'understanding' (*fiqh*) refers to faithful insight. This is man's true core. Armed with this insight, when a believer enters the battlefield, he feels the strength of an army within his single self; he sees God's help to his left and right, and death becomes more beloved to him than life, because his insight reveals before him that station reserved for those who die in the cause of Allah. This insight produces within him that patience and steadfastness which makes him singly heavier than ten of those devoid of this insight.

اَلْئٰنَ خَفَّفَ اللّٰهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ اَنَّ فِيْكُمْ ضَعْفًا ۭفَاِنْ يَّكُن *مِّنْكُمْ مِّائَةٌ صَابِرَةٌ يَّغْلِبُوْا مِائَتَيْنِ ۚ وَاِنْ يَّكُنْ مِّنْكُمْ اَلْفٌ يَّغْلِبُوْٓا اَلْفَيْنِ بِاِذْنِ اللّٰهِ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ مَعَ الصّٰبِرِيْنَ 

**Lessening the Responsibility of the Muslims:** 'Now, Allah has lightened [the hardship] for you, and He knows that among you is weakness.' The word 'now' (*al-āna*) is a grammatical indication that this verse was revealed long after the preceding verses, in the era when the number of Muslims had increased. Thus, the proportion of comparison is also different: in the preceding verse, the comparison was between twenty and two hundred, and one hundred and one thousand; here, it is between one hundred and two hundred, and one thousand and two thousand. This is also proof that this belongs to the era of the Muslims' numerical growth. Since its content belonged to this subject, it was placed here. This also serves as proof of the structural order within the Quran.

While the statement that ten Muslims would overcome one hundred disbelievers was introduced as a glad tiding, it laid a heavy responsibility on the Muslims: that twenty Muslims must consider themselves the match of two hundred disbelievers, and one hundred of one thousand, and must not flee on the excuse of small numbers if they face them in battle. Since fleeing from the battlefield is a grave sin, as stated in verse 16, the Muslims must have felt this responsibility to be highly heavy, attempting to fulfill it with this feeling. Later, when the number of Muslims increased, Allah lightened this burden, replacing the former proportion with that of one hundred to two hundred, and one thousand to two thousand. From the words of the verse, two reasons appear for this change of proportion:

**Two Causes of the Lessening:** First, when Allah's favor increased the number of Muslims, He chose to lighten the heavy burden on the shoulders of the foremost ones (*Sābiqun al-Awwalūn*), laying it on those who came later. Second, those who entered Islam later were not equal in resolve and insight to the foremost ones. Collectively, their standard was lower, which is why He kept their responsibility lighter out of regard for their weakness. This is pointed to in 'He knows that among you is weakness' (*'alima anna fīkum da'fan*). The word 'weakness' (*da'f*) is used not only for physical and material weakness, but also for weakness of resolve, will, knowledge, and insight.

Reflecting on these verses, one reality that becomes clear is that deserving Allah's help is achieved by producing the quality of patience within oneself. Without this quality, no group receives Allah's help. Second, as means and resources increase, God's direct help decreases in proportion. Third, the true strength is the strength of faith; other things are its branches. Fourth, whatever occurs occurs by the command of God; therefore, the core reliance must be on Allah, not on means.

The upcoming subject (Verses 67 to 71): Next, He answered the propaganda the Quraysh spread against Islam, the Muslims, and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) after their defeat at Badr. Before the Battle of Badr, as detailed previously, they used the weakness of Islam and the Muslims as proof against Islam—claiming: 'If this religion were the truth, would it receive such weak and helpless followers? If Muhammad were the Prophet of God, would he be so empty of means and helpers? If Islam were the truth, why does no punishment fall upon us?' In short, they used their dominance and the Muslims' weakness as proof for their being on the truth. They went to the extent of making the Battle of Badr the scale of decision—claiming that whoever won would be considered on the truth, and whoever lost would be considered on falsehood. When the outcome went against them, and they were proven false on their own chosen scale, they had to change their propaganda to save their nation from the effects of the defeat at Badr. Now, they began saying: 'Does any prophet make his own nation fight, causing bloodshed in the land, taking his own brothers as prisoners to collect ransom from them, and looting their wealth to distribute it among his companions?' Their purpose was to claim that these are the deeds of seekers of power and kingship, not of a prophet; therefore, how is he a prophet and what concern does he have with God?

Through this propaganda, the Quraysh sought, on one hand, to save their nation from the effects of the defeat at Badr—lest they accept any concept of the truth of Islam and Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) because of this victory—and, on the other hand, to strike at the Muslims' zeal for Jihad, which had grown after Badr and upon which He had encouraged them in the preceding verses. This made it necessary to answer their propaganda so that no bad effect would fall upon the Muslims. Thus, He answered it by addressing all relevant groups.

First, addressing the Quraysh leaders, He stated: The responsibility of what occurred lies on you, not on the Prophet. No prophet is such that he desires to take prisoners, collect ransom, and cause bloodshed in the land to obtain spoils. These are your desires; God does not desire these things. Be grateful that the matter ended with this. The mischief you committed demanded that a severe punishment fall upon you, but Allah has established a time for everything, which is why He granted you some respite. Afterward, addressing the Muslims, He stated: Do not be affected by the propaganda of these wicked ones. The spoils you obtained are completely lawful and pure for you. In this connection, He sent a message to the prisoners of Badr who were released after paying ransom, telling them not to be grieved by this ransom taken from them. This was a favor shown to them, and if they appreciate this favor, it is highly possible that Allah will bless them with His further favors; but if they do not appreciate it, returning to fight in opposition to Islam, let them remember that they will see harsher days. In this light, recite the verses:

مَا كَانَ لِنَبِيٍّ اَنْ يَّكُوْنَ لَهٗٓ اَسْرٰي حَتّٰي يُثْخِنَ فِي الْاَرْضِ ۭتُرِيْدُوْنَ عَرَضَ الدُّنْيَا ڰ وَاللّٰهُ يُرِيْدُ الْاٰخِرَةَ ۭوَاللّٰهُ عَزِيْزٌ حَكِيْمٌ۔ لَوْلَا كِتٰبٌ مِّنَ اللّٰهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِــيْمَآ اَخَذْتُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيْمٌ 

**The Style of 'Ma Kana' to Absolve From Accusation:** 'It is not for a prophet to have captives [of war] until he inflicts a slaughter [upon Allah's enemies] in the land.' The style of *mā kāna* (it is not for) can be used both to accuse and to absolve from accusation, and both usages are present in the Quran. Defining whether it is for accusation or absolution depends on the context, structure of expression, and the addressee. This exact style is present in Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 161: 'It is not for a prophet to take what does not belong to him (*yaghulla*), and whoever takes what does not belong to him will come with what he took on the Day of Resurrection.' Obviously, this is not an accusation, but is for absolution and purifying the Prophet's status. All commentators agree that this was addressed to the hypocrites, clarifying that the accusation of dishonesty they make against the Prophet is like spitting at the sun; no prophet is such that he would commit dishonesty. In this exact style, the verse under discussion refutes the Quraysh: the accusation you make against the Prophet—claiming that he is seeking power, caused bloodshed in his nation, captured his brothers, looted their wealth, and collected ransom—is merely to cover your own shame. No prophet is such that he would cause bloodshed in the land for the sake of capturing prisoners, collecting ransom, and obtaining spoils. You say this because you measure the Prophet by yourselves. Since these are your desires, you assume the Prophet desires them too.

'You desire the commodities of this world, but Allah desires [for you] the Hereafter.' This address is to the Quraysh. The style of address in the Quran, as we have explained repeatedly, is similar to that of an orator: he addresses all parties present, turning to each in turn based on their state. The words themselves make it clear who the addressee is, and which doubt or objection of theirs is answered. Here, too, is the case. There is no room to assume this is addressed to the Muslims—especially the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and Hazrat Abu Bakr—and even if someone forces themselves to assume so for a moment, how can they muster the heart and soul to assume the subsequent verse is addressed to the Prophet and Abu Bakr?

**The Eloquence of the Address:** In any case, in our view, this address is to the Quraysh, and answers the propaganda to which we pointed. He stated: This desire for the world is your way. Allah desires the Hereafter. The eloquence in this style is worth noting: He did not state that the Prophet and the believers desire the Hereafter, but stated that Allah desires the Hereafter. This is to express the reality that whatever occurs through the Prophet and the believers is not of their own will, but of the will and command of Allah; the Prophet and the believers are merely instruments and means in this task. What they do is indeed the intention and pleasure of Allah. Allah's pleasure for His servants is that they do everything keeping the Hereafter as their target; therefore, how can any action of the Prophet and his companions be contrary to Allah's pleasure? Thus, Allah took the responsibility of Badr and all associated actions upon Himself. Finally, He stated: Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise. He accomplishes whatever He intends, and His every intention is based on justice and wisdom. Now, utter whatever vain talk you wish.

**A Warning for the Future:** 'If not for a decree from Allah that preceded, there would have touched you for what you took a great punishment.' That is: you are raising this wailing over this much. Yet, this is merely a minor blow you suffered. The mischief you committed on this occasion demanded that a great punishment fall upon you; but since Allah has established a time for every nation, before which no final decision is passed, He granted you respite. The meaning is: instead of this wailing, it is better to benefit from this respite to reform your path before the decisive hour arrives.

In 'for what you took' (*fīmā akhadhtum*), 'what' (*mā*) is kept in an aspect of ambiguity, and the verb *akhadha* is used for taking, capturing, adopting, choosing a path, or embarking on a task. In Surah At-Tawbah: 'and if a disaster befalls you, they say, "We took our precaution beforehand."' Here, the meaning is: because of the path you chose, you deserved a great punishment, but under the law of Allah, you received respite.

**Eliminating an Anxiety of Commentators:** Our commentators faced deep anxiety in interpreting these verses. In their view, this is a rebuke to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), Hazrat Abu Bakr, and other Companions for agreeing to take ransom from the prisoners of Badr instead of causing widespread bloodshed in the land. Once the correct interpretation is clear, there is no need to refute this view, but keep several things in mind. First, even if an error was committed by the Prophet and the Companions in accepting the ransom, it was not a violation of any prior prohibition, but was an error of judgment (*ijtihad*). An error of judgment is not something for which such a severe threat is revealed—especially an error of judgment confirmed immediately by Allah Himself. Second, it was not an error of judgment either. The law regarding prisoners of war had been explained in Surah Muhammad before this: that they may be killed, released after paying ransom, or released purely as a favor. Third, as far as bloodshed is concerned, there was no lack of it at Badr. Seventy of the Quraysh, including major leaders, were killed, and around the same number captured. The rest of the army fled; with whom then was the battle to be continued? Fourth, the words of rebuke used here are specific to the Quran. Anyone familiar with the Quran's style knows that in these words, the Quran has rebuked only the disbelievers and hypocrites. For whoever has doubt, let him check all places in the Quran where 'If not for a decree from Allah...' is used to rebuke someone.

فَكُلُوْا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلٰلًا طَيِّبًا ڮ وَّاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ 

**Assuring the Lawfulness of the Spoils:** Now, He turned to the Muslims, assuring them: Pay no heed to their vain talk; eat and use the spoils and ransom you obtained, for they are completely lawful and pure for you. Since this statement is part of the defense of the Muslims, it was coordinate (*'atf*) with the preceding verses through the particle *fa*. The only difference is that the preceding statement was addressed to the Quraysh, because it belonged to them, and this second statement turned to the Muslims, because it belonged to them. Such subtle shifts in the address are common in the Quran; indeed, multiple examples have passed in this Surah. A beautiful example is in Surah Yusuf: '"Yوسف, ignore this. And, [O wife], ask forgiveness for your sin. Indeed, you were of the sinful."' (Yusuf: 29). Look: in a single breath, the Ruler of Egypt addressed Yusuf and his wife, and the shift of focus and the nature of the statement made the difference in address clear without any confusion. Here, the assurance given to the Muslims regarding the lawfulness and purity of the spoils is in response to the Quraysh. We pointed to this previously: after Badr, the Quraysh spread the propaganda that the Muslims claim piety, yet their deeds are those of seekers of the world—causing bloodshed, capturing prisoners, obtaining spoils, and eating it. The Quran showed: Pay no heed to their verdicts. In their eyes, you are wrong under all circumstances. If you had lost, they would have used your defeat as proof for your being on falsehood; now that you won, they use your capturing prisoners, obtaining spoils, and collecting ransom as proof for your being on falsehood. The way to deal with them is to ignore them and benefit from the favors God blessed you with; they are lawful and pure for you.

It should be kept in mind that in that era, the monastic concept of religion was dominant; therefore, the possibility could not be ignored that some good-hearted people might be affected by this propaganda, which would affect the Muslims' zeal for Jihad. The Quran refuted them, preventing this possibility.

'and fear Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.' The meaning is: what is lawful and pure, eat and use; but fear Allah lest you fall into something He prohibited. If you avoid major sins, He will not apprehend you for minor lapses, as He is Forgiving and Merciful.

**Addressing the Prisoners of the Battle of Badr:** 'O Prophet, say to whoever is in your hands of the captives, "If Allah knows [any] good in your hearts, He will give you [something] better than what was taken from you, and He will forgive you; and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."' This is a message alongside a threat to the prisoners of Badr. The message is: instead of being grieved by the ransom taken from you, you must be grateful to Allah and His Messenger that you were released after paying ransom instead of being killed. This is a great favor shown to you, and the way to appreciate this favor is to evaluate your path with an open mind, reflecting on the entire matter in the light of intellect and justice instead of emotions. If you do so, you will appreciate the favor, and this will attract Allah's mercy and facilitation toward you, and He will bless you with the gift of Islam, which is far better than the ransom taken from you, and will forgive you.

وَاِنْ يُّرِيْدُوْا خِيَانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خَانُوا اللّٰهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَاَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ ۭوَاللّٰهُ عَلِيْمٌ حَكِيْمٌ 

This is consolation to the Prophet and a threat to the prisoners. Addressing the Prophet, He stated: If they choose unfaithfulness, failing to appreciate the favor you showed them, and return to fight, they will not harm you in the least, but will invite their own ruin. Before this, they chose unfaithfulness and breach of covenant toward God, and they tasted its result: God put them in your power. If they commit this again, God will put them in your power again, and they will face the punishment. The breach of covenant referred to here was detailed previously—namely: He made them the guardians of His Sanctuary and entrusted them with the inheritance of Abraham, but they desecrate its sanctity and distorted Abraham's religion, because of which their punishment is coming. If they wish to add further to their crimes, let them; they will taste its fruits.

Reflecting on these two verses (verses 70-71), it becomes clear that the Prophet's releasing the prisoners after taking ransom was not only not objected to by Allah, but He liked it, sending a message to these prisoners that this was done so that if they appreciate the favor, the way to embrace Islam and seek forgiveness would open for them. Reflect: where is this, and where is that which commentators chose based on certain historical narrations—claiming that the Prophet was rebuked because he took prisoners and accepted ransom before causing widespread bloodshed?

The upcoming subject (Verses 72 to 75): Now, these are the concluding verses of the Surah. Muslims are commanded to organize on the basis of faith and emigration, making faith and emigration the foundation of mutual alliance and support. The relationships and responsibilities of the times of ignorance were ended. It was decreed: those who believe, emigrate to Medina, and strive alongside the Muslims against the disbelievers are a single nation, and brothers and protectors to one another. Thus, the Islamic society was commanded to organize on its specific foundations, so the Ansar and Mahajirun would stand as a solid structure against the forces of disbelief. The encouragement of Jihad given throughout the Surah is a preparation for this, and serves as an introduction to the declaration of disassociation from the disbelievers that is coming in the subsequent Surah. Recite the verses:

اِنَّ الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَهَاجَرُوْا وَجٰهَدُوْا بِاَمْوَالِهِمْ وَاَنْفُسِهِمْ فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰوَوْا وَّنَــصَرُوْٓا اُولٰۗىِٕكَ بَعْضُهُمْ اَوْلِيَاۗءُ بَعْضٍ ۭ وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَلَمْ يُهَاجِرُوْا مَا لَكُمْ مِّنْ وَّلَايَتِهِمْ مِّنْ شَيْءٍ حَتّٰي يُهَاجِرُوْا ۚ وَاِنِ اسْتَنْصَرُوْكُمْ فِي الدِّيْنِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ النَّصْرُ اِلَّا عَلٰي قَوْمٍۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ مِّيْثَاقٌ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُوْنَ بَصِيْرٌ 

**In Islam, Support and Help are Based on Faith:** Before Islam, mutual support and help were based on family and tribal bias (*'asabiyyah*). If a person or family fell into danger or calamity, their family or tribe would rise to defend them. In the new society established in Medina, Islam based support and help on faith and emigration. He stated: Those who believed, emigrated, and strove with their wealth and lives in the cause of Allah, and those who sheltered them and helped them—these are protectors and allies to one another. 'Those who believed and emigrated' refers to the Muhajirun, and 'those who sheltered and helped' refers to the Ansar. These two groups are mentioned not through their names, but through their attributes and services. This points to the fact that in this new society, instead of family and lineage bias, what is valid is faith, Islam, emigration, and Jihad. These are protectors and allies to one another. Obviously, this support and help is against the forces of disbelief. Although this is expressed in the style of stating a fact, it carries the meaning of a command—namely: the believers must stand as protectors and helpers to one another against the forces of disbelief, defending each other when the need arises.

'But those who believed and did not emigrate - for you there is no guardianship of them at all until they emigrate.' Those who believed but did not emigrate from the land of disbelief to Medina, the land of Islam, were excluded from this alliance of protection—meaning: the residents of the land of Islam do not carry the responsibility of protecting, helping, and defending them. The reason for not carrying this responsibility was that practically, it was impossible, and would have created many international complications. Furthermore, at that time, the interest and the command demanded that all those who believe exit from the land of disbelief to gather in Medina, so they could organize to face the forces of disbelief and liberate the Kaaba.

'And if they ask victory of you for the religion, then you must aid, except against a people between whom and you is a treaty.' **Respecting Treaties:** That is: although the residents of the land of Islam carry no responsibility to defend those who did not emigrate, if they seek help specifically for the sake of the religion, you must provide whatever help is possible, on the condition that this help is not directed against a nation with whom you have a treaty. Respecting treaties is primary. 'And Allah is Seer of what you do' is to emphasize the respect for treaties. The meaning is: whatever step you take, secretly or openly, contrary to the respect for treaties, God is not ignorant of it. He sees everything.

وَالَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا بَعْضُهُمْ اَوْلِيَاۗءُ بَعْضٍ ۭاِلَّا تَفْعَلُوْهُ تَكُنْ فِتْنَةٌ فِي الْاَرْضِ وَفَسَادٌ كَبِيْرٌ 

**The Reason for Helping the Muslims in the Land of Disbelief for the Religion:** This explains why helping the Muslims in the land of disbelief specifically for the sake of the religion is necessary: because as far as hostility toward Islam and the Muslims is concerned, all the disbelievers have become allies to one another. Whichever servant of Allah accepts Islam, persecuting and torturing him is considered a good deed by all of them. To the extent that even the family bias of his relatives is dead to save him from their oppression. His wealth and life are completely lawful to them. Under such circumstances, if you do not help these oppressed ones, it means that trial—forcing people away from their religion—will become common throughout the land. *Fitnah* is used in the sense of persecution, and the referent of the object pronoun in *illā taf'alūhu* is that help mentioned in 'then you must aid'.

وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَهَاجَرُوْا وَجٰهَدُوْا فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰوَوْا وَّنَصَرُوْٓا اُولٰۗىِٕكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُوْنَ حَقًّا ۭ لَهُمْ مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَّرِزْقٌ كَرِيْمٌ 

**Emigration is the Criterion of Truth for Muslims in the Land of Disbelief:** This verse designates emigration as the criterion of truth for Muslims in the land of disbelief. When He stated that the true and sincere believers are those who emigrated and strove, and those who sheltered them and helped them, it naturally emerged that the true core of Islam consists of the Mahajirun and Ansar. As for those who believe but have not emigrated from the land of disbelief, to prove the truth of their faith, they must exit from the land of disbelief to join the land of Islam, striving alongside the Mahajirun and Ansar. This also clarified that the primary purpose of emigration was to organize the Muslims for Jihad, while subtly indicating that it also serves as a criterion to distinguish between faith and hypocrisy. Consequently, in the subsequent Surah (Surah At-Tawbah), it will be clear that those who, despite claiming faith, avoided emigration until the end without any valid excuse were counted among the hypocrites.

وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا مِنْۢ بَعْدُ وَهَاجَرُوْا وَجٰهَدُوْا مَعَكُمْ فَاُولٰۗىِٕكَ مِنْكُمْ ۭ وَاُولُوا الْاَعْرَابِ بَعْضُهُمْ اَوْلٰى بِبَعْضٍ فِيْ كِتٰبِ اللّٰهِ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيْمٌ 

This encourages the Muslims in the land of disbelief to emigrate—showing that the door of this Islamic society is open to you; race toward it and find your rank within it—while reminding the Muslims in the land of Islam to keep their hearts open to those who believe and emigrate later, as they are also your brothers and parts of your collective body. Let no feeling of superiority arise within you against them, nor any narrowness in your hearts.

'But those of kindred are closer to one another in the decree of Allah.' **The Shariah Rights of Kindred are Primary:** Alongside the general rule of brotherhood and alliance mentioned above, He recalled the specific law of willed rights and inheritance described in the Quran—meaning: those rights established by Allah based on blood relationships will remain established. This brotherhood will not alter them. The restriction 'in the decree of Allah' (*fī kitābi Allāhi*) alongside the rights of kindred clarifies that 'rights' here refers to those rights of relatives and womb described in the law of Allah, excluding those customs of ignorance that had been common. In other places in the Quran, where Muslims are emphasized to show goodness and support to their religious brothers and allies, He warned that the Shariah rights of kindred must remain primary. In Surah Al-Ahzab: 'And those of kindred are closer to one another in the decree of Allah than the [other] believers and the Emigrants, except that you may do to your close allies some kindness. That is in the Book written.' (Al-Ahzab: 6).

'Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things.' That is: every command of Allah is based on His perfect knowledge, and He has designated a specific place and position for everything. Religious brotherhood and alliance have their sphere, and blood relationship has its place. Respect both in their respective places, and perform those rights Allah has established for them.

**Some Principles of Islamic Politics:** Reflecting on this group of verses (verses 72 to 75), several principles of Islamic politics—both national and international—emerge. We point to them briefly:

1. The Ansar and Mahajirun are allies of one another; between them exists the link of faith and emigration, and based on it, the rights and duties of brotherhood and alliance are established. The past familial and tribal biases are ended, and mutual support and defense are established on the basis of Islamic brotherhood.
2. Those who believe but did not emigrate from the land of disbelief to the land of Islam do not share in these rights of protection and alliance of the new Islamic society, until they emigrate.
3. If these Muslims in the land of disbelief are persecuted because of their embracing Islam, they must be helped to save them from oppression, provided this help does not require fighting a nation with whom you have a treaty.
4. Emigration was made obligatory upon every Muslim so they could gather and organize in one center to face the forces of disbelief.
5. The rights Allah established based on blood relationships will not be affected by the rights of Islamic brotherhood; they will remain primary under all circumstances.

Upon these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. And our final call is: Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
